title.zh: 卷八十一 匡張孔馬傳
title.en: Volume 81: Kuang, Zhang, Kong and Ma
title.raw: 卷八十一 匡張孔馬傳 Volume 81: Kuang, Zhang, Kong and Ma

sentences.zh: 匡衡，字稚圭，東海承人也。

translations.literal: Kuang Heng, courtesy name Zhigui, was a native of Cheng in Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Kuang Heng, style Zhigui, came from Cheng in Donghai commandery.

sentences.zh: 父世農夫，至衡好學，家貧，庸作以供資用，尤精力過絕人。

translations.literal: His father for generations had been a farmer; by the time of Heng he loved learning, the family was poor, and he hired out his labor to supply expenses—his vigor and concentration especially surpassed other men.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears tilled the soil; Heng loved books, but poverty forced him to work for hire while his focus and stamina outstripped everyone around him.

sentences.zh: 諸儒為之語曰：「無說《詩》，匡鼎來；

translations.literal: The scholars made a saying about him: “If you will not lecture on the Odes, here comes Kuang Ding;
translations.idiomatic: The Ru had a rhyme: “Lecture not on the Odes—and here comes Kuang Ding;

sentences.zh: 匡語《詩》，解人頤。」

translations.literal: When Kuang discourses on the Odes, it unlocks people’s chins with delight.”
translations.idiomatic: Let Kuang expound the Odes, and hearers grin in spite of themselves.”

sentences.zh: 衡射策甲科，以不應令除為太常掌故，調補平原文學。

translations.literal: Heng placed in the first class of the graded examination answers but, because he did not meet the ordinance, was appointed Archivist of the Chamberlain for Ceremonial and transferred to fill the post of literary scholar in Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: He topped the policy exam yet missed a technical rule, so he was posted as a Grand Master of Ceremonies archivist, then transferred to be literary instructor in Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 學者多上書薦衡經明，當世少雙，令為文學就官京師；

translations.literal: Scholars in numbers submitted memorials recommending Heng as lucid in the classics, without peer in the age, ordering him as literary scholar to take office at the capital;
translations.idiomatic: Classicists petitioned in droves that Heng’s mastery of the canon was unrivaled and that he should be summoned to the capital as a court scholar;

sentences.zh: 後進皆欲從衡平原，衡不宜在遠方。

translations.literal: later students all wished to follow Heng to Pingyuan—Heng ought not to remain in a distant place.
translations.idiomatic: students everywhere wanted to study under him in Pingyuan, and argued that he should not be kept in the provinces.

sentences.zh: 事下太子太傅蕭望之、少府梁丘賀問，衡對《詩》諸大義，其對深美。

translations.literal: The matter was sent to Heir Apparent Grand Tutor Xiao Wangzhi and Privy Treasurer Liangqiu He for questioning; Heng answered on the great meanings of the Odes, and his replies were deep and excellent.
translations.idiomatic: The case went to Xiao Wangzhi and Liangqiu He, who examined him; his exposition of the Odes was profound and polished.

sentences.zh: 望之奏衡經學精習，說有師道，可觀覽。

translations.literal: Wangzhi memorialized that Heng’s classical learning was refined and practiced, that his teaching followed a master’s path, and that he was worth employing.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi reported that Heng’s scholarship was thorough, his instruction sound, and that the throne should take notice.

sentences.zh: 宣帝不甚用儒，遣衡歸官。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan did not much use Confucians and sent Heng back to his official post.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan had little use for Ru learning and sent him back to his provincial post.

sentences.zh: 而皇太子見衡對，私善之。

translations.literal: But the heir apparent, having seen Heng’s answers, privately thought well of him.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince alone, having heard his answers, kept him in mind with favor.

sentences.zh: 是時，有日蝕、地震之變，上問以政治得失，衡上疏曰：

translations.literal: At this time there were changes of solar eclipse and earthquake; the emperor asked concerning gains and losses in government, and Heng submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Then came eclipse and earthquake; the emperor asked for counsel on policy, and Heng memorialized:

sentences.zh: 臣聞五帝不同禮，三王各異教，民俗殊務，所遇之時異也。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that the Five Emperors had different ritual, the Three Kings each had different teaching, the people’s customs had different tasks—the times they met were not the same.
translations.idiomatic: The Five Thearchs did not share one rite, nor the Three Kings one teaching: customs shift because ages differ.

sentences.zh: 陛下躬聖德，開太平之路，閔愚吏民觸法抵禁，比年大赦，使百姓得改行自新，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Your Majesty in person embodies sagely virtue, opens the road to great peace, pities foolish clerks and commoners who violate law and strike prohibitions, and in successive years has greatly pardoned, enabling the hundred surnames to change conduct and make themselves new—exceedingly fortunate for all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: You embody sage virtue, seek great peace, pity offenders, and year after year grant sweeping amnesties so the people may start anew—blessing indeed.

sentences.zh: 臣竊見大赦之後，奸邪不為衰止，今日大赦，明日犯法，相隨入獄，此殆導之未得其務也。

translations.literal: Your subject has privately seen that after great pardons wickedness and deviance have not declined and stopped; today a great pardon, tomorrow violation of law—following one another into prison—this is nearly because guidance has not hit the right task.
translations.idiomatic: Yet after each amnesty crime does not ebb; men pardoned today are jailed tomorrow—surely we are not guiding them aright.

sentences.zh: 蓋保民者，「陳之以德義」，「示之以好惡」，觀其失而制其宜，故動之而和，綏之而安。

translations.literal: For protecting the people one must “set forth virtue and righteousness to them,” “show them what to love and what to hate,” observe their failures and regulate what is fitting—therefore when they are stirred they harmonize, when they are soothed they are secure.
translations.idiomatic: To guard the people the classic says: teach them with duty and right, show them love and loathing, read their faults and set fitting norms—then motion brings harmony and ease brings calm.

sentences.zh: 今天下俗貪財賤義，好聲色，上侈靡，廉恥之節薄，淫辟之意縱，綱紀失序，疏者逾內，親戚之恩薄，婚姻之黨隆，苟合僥倖，以身設利。

translations.literal: Now custom under heaven esteems wealth and despises duty, loves sound and color, the superior is extravagant, the restraint of integrity and shame is thin, thoughts of excess and depravity run free, the net of law loses order, the distant overstep the inner, kindness to kin grows thin while marriage factions swell, men join in reckless hope and set their persons to seek profit.
translations.idiomatic: Today the world worships gold, scorns integrity, chases pleasure, and apes extravagance above; shame thins, lust swells, law frays, outsiders trump kin, in-laws grow strong, and men gamble body and soul for gain.

sentences.zh: 不改其原，雖歲赦之，刑猶難使錯而不用也。

translations.literal: If one does not reform this root, though one pardons every year, punishments will still be hard to set aside and not use.
translations.idiomatic: Unless the root is healed, yearly pardons will never idle the executioner’s axe.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為宜一曠然大變其俗。

translations.literal: Your subject foolishly thinks it fitting to make one vast, clear great change in these customs.
translations.idiomatic: I urge one sweeping reform of custom from the top down.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「能以禮讓為國乎，何有？」

translations.literal: Confucius said, “If one could use ritual yielding to govern a state, what difficulty would there be?”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius asked, “Who could govern a state if ritual and deference were its rule?”

sentences.zh: 朝廷者，天下之楨□也。

translations.literal: The court is the pillar and frame of all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The court is the empire’s backbone.

sentences.zh: 公卿大夫相與循禮恭讓，則民不爭；

translations.literal: If dukes, ministers, and grandees together follow ritual and are reverent in yielding, then the people will not contend;
translations.idiomatic: When high ministers walk in ritual and bow to one another, the people cease to quarrel;

sentences.zh: 好仁樂施，則下不暴；

translations.literal: if they love humaneness and delight in giving, then those below will not be violent;
translations.idiomatic: when they cherish kindness and love to give, those below shed violence;

sentences.zh: 上義高節，則民興行；

translations.literal: if they esteem duty and prize high integrity, then the people will rise to conduct;
translations.idiomatic: when they honor right and hold fast to integrity, the people rise to virtue;

sentences.zh: 寬柔和惠，則眾相愛。

translations.literal: if they are lenient, gentle, and kind, then the multitude will love one another.
translations.idiomatic: when they are mild, soft, and generous, the multitude love one another.

sentences.zh: 四者，明王之所以不嚴而成化也。

translations.literal: These four are how an enlightened king completes transformation without severity.
translations.idiomatic: These four are how a wise king transforms the realm without harshness.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why is that?

sentences.zh: 朝有變色之言，則下有爭鬥之患；

translations.literal: If in court there are words that change color, then below there is the calamity of quarrel and strife;
translations.idiomatic: When courtiers snarl at one another, the folk take up the brawl;

sentences.zh: 上有自專之士，則下有不讓之人；

translations.literal: if above there are men who act on private judgment, then below there are men who do not yield;
translations.idiomatic: when those above insist on their own way, those below refuse deference;

sentences.zh: 上有克勝之佐，則下有傷害之心；

translations.literal: if above there are assistants who overcome by force, then below there are hearts that harm and injure;
translations.idiomatic: when those above prize crushing rivals, those below learn to wound;

sentences.zh: 上有好利之臣，則下有盜竊之民：此其本也。

translations.literal: if above there are ministers who love profit, then below there are people who steal: this is the root.
translations.idiomatic: when those above chase gain, theft spreads below—that is the root.

sentences.zh: 今俗吏之治，皆不本禮讓，而上克暴，或忮害好陷人於罪，貪財而慕勢，故犯法者眾，奸邪不止，雖嚴刑峻法，猶不為變。

translations.literal: Now the governance of vulgar clerks none roots in ritual yielding but all press to overcome and be violent, or are jealous and injurious and delight in trapping men in guilt, covet wealth and admire power—therefore those who violate law are many, wickedness and deviance do not stop; though there be harsh punishments and strict laws, still they do not change.
translations.idiomatic: Petty officials today ignore ritual and deference, bully and scheme, jail men for sport, and worship wealth and power—so crime multiplies and no severity of law will cure it.

sentences.zh: 此非其天性，有由然也。

translations.literal: This is not their heaven-nature; there is a reason it is so.
translations.idiomatic: That is not fate; it comes from example.

sentences.zh: 臣竊考《國風》之詩，《周南》、《召南》被賢聖之化深，故篤於行而廉於色。

translations.literal: Your subject has privately examined the poems of the “Airs of the States”: “South of Zhou” and “South of Shao” were steeped in worthy and sage transformation, therefore they were steadfast in conduct and restrained in matters of color.
translations.idiomatic: In the Airs, Zhou and Shao absorbed the deepest sage teaching, so their people were steady in deed and chaste in passion.

sentences.zh: 鄭伯好勇，而國人暴虎；

translations.literal: The Earl of Zheng loved courage and the people of the state wrestled tigers bare-handed;
translations.idiomatic: Duke Zhuang of Zheng loved valor, and his people faced tigers with bare hands;

sentences.zh: 秦穆貴信，而士多從死；

translations.literal: Duke Mu of Qin prized good faith and many gentlemen followed him in death;
translations.idiomatic: Duke Mu of Qin honored sworn word, and many retainers followed him into the grave;

sentences.zh: 陳夫人好巫，而民淫祀；

translations.literal: The lady of Chen loved shamans and the people indulged in licentious sacrifice;
translations.idiomatic: The duchess of Chen doted on witchcraft, and licentious shrines multiplied;

sentences.zh: 晉侯好儉，而民畜聚；

translations.literal: Marquis Wen of Jin loved frugality and the people hoarded and gathered;
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Wen of Jin preached thrift, and his people hid every grain;

sentences.zh: 太王躬仁，邠國貴恕。

translations.literal: Great King Dan in person practiced humaneness and in Bin the state prized forgiveness.
translations.idiomatic: King Tai embodied kindness, and Bin learned forbearance.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，治天下者審所上而已。

translations.literal: Viewed from this, those who order all under heaven need only examine what they set above.
translations.idiomatic: He who would rule the world need only watch what he lifts on high.

sentences.zh: 今之偽薄忮害，不讓極矣。

translations.literal: Now falseness, meanness, jealousy, and injury, and refusal to yield have reached the extreme.
translations.idiomatic: Today hypocrisy, malice, and strife have passed all bounds.

sentences.zh: 臣聞教化之流，非家至而人說之也。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that the flow of teaching and transformation does not reach every house and persuade every person.
translations.idiomatic: Moral sway does not visit each door; it rides on example.

sentences.zh: 賢者在位，能者布職，朝廷崇禮，百僚敬讓，道德之行，由內及外，自近者始，然後民知所法，遷善日進而不自知。

translations.literal: When the worthy are in place and the capable fill their posts, when the court esteems ritual and the hundred officials revere yielding, the practice of virtue and power travels from within to without, beginning with what is near—then the people know what to model, move toward goodness day by day without knowing it.
translations.idiomatic: Put worth in office, skill in duty, exalt ritual at court, and let ministers defer—virtue then radiates outward, the people catch the pattern, and improve unaware.

sentences.zh: 是以百姓安，陰陽和，神靈應，而嘉祥見。

translations.literal: Therefore the hundred surnames are secure, yin and yang harmonize, spirits respond, and auspicious omens appear.
translations.idiomatic: Then the people rest easy, yin and yang balance, the spirits answer, and good omens come.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：「商邑翼翼，四方之極；

translations.literal: The Odes says, “The capital of Shang stands in awe, the pole-star of the four quarters;”
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry says, “Shang’s capital stood ordered, a polestar for every quarter;”

sentences.zh: 壽考且寧，以保我後生」此成湯所以建至治，保子孫，化異俗而懷鬼方也。

translations.literal: “long life and moreover tranquility, thereby to protect our later-born”—this is how accomplished Tang built utmost good order, guarded sons and grandsons, transformed alien customs, and cherished the Gui Fang.
translations.idiomatic: “Long life and peace to guard our heirs”—such was how Tang built perfect rule, secured posterity, tamed barbarians, and drew the Gui Fang to him.

sentences.zh: 今長安天子之都，親承聖化，然其習俗無以異於遠方，郡國來者無所法則，或見侈靡而放效之。

translations.literal: Now Chang’an is the capital of the Son of Heaven, personally receiving sagely transformation, yet its habits and customs are no different from distant regions; those who come from commanderies and kingdoms have nothing to take as model, or they see extravagance and imitate it.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Chang’an, though it drinks your teaching, looks no better than the frontier; envoys find no model here, only excess to copy.

sentences.zh: 此教化之原本，風俗之樞機，宜先正者也。

translations.literal: This is the root source of teaching and transformation, the pivot of customs—it is what ought to be corrected first.
translations.idiomatic: That is the wellspring of reform and the hinge of custom—it must be set right first.

sentences.zh: 臣聞天人之際，精祲有以相蕩，善惡有以相推，事作乎下者象動乎上，陰陽之理各應其感，陰變則靜者動，陽蔽則明者暗，水旱之災隨類而至。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that at the juncture of heaven and man subtle essences have means to strike one another, good and evil have means to push one another; when affairs arise below, images move above—the principle of yin and yang each responds to what stirs it; when yin shifts, what was still moves; when yang is blocked, what was bright grows dark; disasters of flood and drought arrive according to their kind.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven and man answer each other: deeds below stir signs above; yin and yang echo every impulse—shift yin and stillness quakes, block yang and light fails; flood and drought follow their class.

sentences.zh: 今關東連年饑饉，百姓乏困，或至相食，此皆生於賦斂多，民所共者大，而吏安集之不稱之效也。

translations.literal: Now east of the Pass successive years of famine have left the hundred surnames destitute, some even to eating one another—all this arises from heavy levies, the great burden shared by the people, and the effect of clerks not fittingly settling and gathering them.
translations.idiomatic: East of the Pass famine has driven men to cannibalism—taxes are crushing, the burden universal, and magistrates fail to bring relief.

sentences.zh: 陛下祗畏天戒，哀閔元元，大自減損，省甘泉、建章官衛，罷珠崖，偃武行文，將欲度唐、虞之隆，絕殷、周之衰也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty reverently fears heaven’s warnings, pities the black-haired people, has greatly reduced and cut yourself, lessened the guards at Sweet Springs and Jianzhang, abolished Pearl Cliff, laid down arms and raised culture, intending to measure up to the height of Tang and Yu and cut off the decline of Yin and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: You fear heaven’s rebuke, pity the people, cut court expense, thinned the Sweet Springs and Jianzhang guards, abandoned Zhuya, sheathed swords for writing brushes, aiming to rival Yao and Shun and end Shang-Zhou decay.

sentences.zh: 諸見罷珠崖詔書者，莫不欣欣，人自以將見太平也。

translations.literal: Of those who saw the edict abolishing Pearl Cliff, none were not joyful; each person thought he would soon see great peace.
translations.idiomatic: All who read the edict ending the Zhuya campaign rejoiced, each believing peace was near.

sentences.zh: 宜遂減官室之度，省靡麗之飾，考制度，修外內，近忠正，遠巧佞，放鄭、衛，進《雅》、《頌》，舉異材，開直言，任溫良之人，退刻薄之吏，顯潔白之士，昭無慾之路，覽《六藝》之意，察上世之務，明自然之道，博和睦之化，以崇至仁，匡失俗，易民視，令海內昭然咸見本朝之所貴，道德弘於京師，淑問揚乎疆外，然後大化可成，禮讓可興也。

translations.literal: It is fitting then to reduce the scale of palaces and halls, cut lavish ornament, examine institutions, repair inner and outer, draw near the loyal and upright, distance the crafty and flattering, dismiss the sounds of Zheng and Wei, advance the Ya and Song, raise unusual talents, open straight speech, employ the warm and good, retire harsh clerks, display the pure and white, illumine the road without desire, survey the meaning of the Six Arts, inspect the tasks of high antiquity, clarify the way of nature, broadly spread the transformation of harmony, thereby to honor utmost humaneness, rectify failed customs, change the people’s outlook, cause all within the seas clearly to see what this court esteems, let moral power spread wide at the capital and fair report shine beyond the borders—then great transformation can be completed and ritual yielding can rise.
translations.idiomatic: Next, shrink palace scale, strip vain display, audit rites, mend court and countryside, trust the loyal, banish flatterers, silence Zheng and Wei airs, lift the Ya and Song, call forth talent, invite blunt counsel, promote the gentle, sack the cruel, honor the clean, open the path of restraint, study the Six Arts, ponder high antiquity, read nature’s pattern, spread concord, crown all with humaneness, mend custom, and refashion every eye until the realm sees what this dynasty prizes, virtue fills the capital, and good name crosses the frontier—then true peace and deference may rise.

sentences.zh: 上說其言，遷衡為光祿大夫、太子少傅。

translations.literal: The emperor was pleased with his words and promoted Heng to Palace Grandee and Junior Tutor to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved and made him palace grandee and junior tutor to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 時，上好儒術文辭，頗改宣帝之政，言事者多進見，人人自以為得上意。

translations.literal: At this time the emperor favored Confucian learning and literary phrasing, had considerably altered Emperor Xuan’s policies, and many who spoke on affairs advanced to audience—each man thought he had hit the emperor’s intention.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then doted on Ru letters; he had shifted Xuan’s course, and every petitioner fancied he had read the ruler’s mind.

sentences.zh: 又傅昭儀及子定陶王愛幸，寵於皇后、太子。

translations.literal: Moreover Lady Fu the Brilliant Companion and her son the King of Dingtao were loved and favored, outranking the empress and heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Lady Fu and her son the king of Dingtao won favor that eclipsed empress and heir.

sentences.zh: 衡復上疏曰：

translations.literal: Heng again submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Heng memorialized again:

sentences.zh: 臣聞治亂安危之機，在乎審所用心。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that the pivot of order and chaos, safety and danger, lies in examining where the mind is applied.
translations.idiomatic: Order and chaos turn on where the ruler fixes his mind.

sentences.zh: 蓋受命之王務在創業垂統傳之無窮，繼體之君心存於承宣先王之德而褒大其功。

translations.literal: For a king who receives the mandate the task lies in founding the enterprise and handing down the thread to endless transmission; for a lord who continues the body the mind rests in receiving and proclaiming the former kings’ virtue and magnifying their merit.
translations.idiomatic: Founders must build a line to last; heirs must broadcast the virtue of their fathers and magnify their deeds.

sentences.zh: 昔者成王之嗣位，思述文、武之道以養其心，休烈盛美皆歸之二後而不敢專其名，是以上天歆享，鬼神祐焉。

translations.literal: In antiquity when King Cheng succeeded, he thought to pursue the way of Wen and Wu to nourish his heart; splendid merit and great beauty he all attributed to the two queens and did not dare monopolize the name—therefore high heaven rejoiced and shared, and ghosts and spirits protected him.
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng fed his heart on Wen and Wu, credited both queens for every glory, claimed no fame himself—so heaven feasted him and spirits shielded his reign.

sentences.zh: 其《詩》曰：「念我皇祖，陟降廷止。」

translations.literal: Its Odes says, “Think on our august ancestors, who ascend and descend in the court.”
translations.idiomatic: The Odes has it: “Think of our royal ancestors; they mount and alight in this hall.”

sentences.zh: 言成王常思祖考之業，而鬼神祐助其治也。

translations.literal: It means King Cheng always thought on the work of ancestors and forbears, and ghosts and spirits aided his rule.
translations.idiomatic: That is King Cheng ever mindful of his forbears, whom heaven aided in turn.

sentences.zh: 陛下聖德天覆，子愛海內，然陰陽未和，奸邪未禁者，殆論議者未丕揚先帝之盛功，爭言制度不可用也，務變更之，所更或不可行，而復復之，是以群下更相是非，吏民無所信。

translations.literal: Your Majesty’s sagely virtue is heaven’s covering and childlike love within the seas, yet yin and yang are not yet harmonious and wickedness is not yet forbidden—nearly because debaters have not greatly proclaimed the former emperor’s splendid merit, wrangle that institutions cannot be used, insist on changing them, what is changed sometimes cannot be practiced, then is changed back—therefore those below wrangle back and forth over right and wrong, and clerks and commoners have nothing to trust.
translations.idiomatic: Your virtue blankets the realm, yet portents still clash and vice runs free—because court talkers slight the late emperor’s achievements, call old law useless, and churn endless reform until no one knows what to believe.

sentences.zh: 臣竊恨國家釋樂成之業，而虛為此紛紛也。

translations.literal: Your subject privately regrets that the state has set aside a completed work of joy and vainly made this hubbub.
translations.idiomatic: I grieve that we abandon a finished harmony for empty turmoil.

sentences.zh: 願陛下詳覽統業之事，留神於遵制揚功，以定群下之心。

translations.literal: I wish Your Majesty would carefully survey the business of the unified enterprise and fix attention on following institutions and raising merit, thereby to settle the minds of those below.
translations.idiomatic: Study the whole patrimony, hold fast to precedent, trumpet past merit, and steady every heart below.

sentences.zh: 《大雅》曰：「無念爾祖，聿修厥德。」

translations.literal: The Da Ya says, “Do not fix your thoughts on your ancestors; pursue and cultivate their virtue.”
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Ya says, “Forget not your ancestors; build their virtue anew.”

sentences.zh: 孔子著之《孝經》首章，蓋至德之本也。

translations.literal: Confucius placed it in the first chapter of the Classic of Filial Piety—this is the root of utmost virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius set those words at the head of the Classic of Filial Piety—the taproot of supreme virtue.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「審好惡，理情性，而王道畢矣。」

translations.literal: The tradition says, “Examine loves and hates, regulate emotion and nature, and the kingly way is complete.”
translations.idiomatic: The tradition runs: “Sort love from hate, train temper, and the kingly way is whole.”

sentences.zh: 能盡其性，然後能盡人物之性；

translations.literal: When one can exhaust one’s nature, then one can exhaust the nature of other persons and things;
translations.idiomatic: Only he who perfects his own nature can sound the nature of others;

sentences.zh: 能盡人物之性，可以贊天地之化。

translations.literal: when one can exhaust the nature of persons and things, one can assist the transforming work of heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: who sounds the nature of things may assist heaven and earth in their making.

sentences.zh: 治性之道，必審已之所有餘，而強其所不足。

translations.literal: The way to govern nature must examine what one has in surplus and strengthen what one lacks.
translations.idiomatic: To master oneself, mark your excess and shore your lack.

sentences.zh: 蓋聰明疏通者戒於大察，寡聞少見者戒於雍蔽，勇猛剛強者戒於大暴，仁愛溫良者戒於無斷，湛靜安舒者戒於後時，廣心浩大者戒於遺忘。

translations.literal: For the bright and penetrating, the warning is against too keen scrutiny; for the narrow-heard and seldom-seeing, the warning is against blockage; for the brave and hard, the warning is against great violence; for the humane and warm and good, the warning is against lack of decision; for the deep, still, calm, and easy, the warning is against lateness; for the broad-minded and vast, the warning is against forgetfulness.
translations.idiomatic: The quick must beware hypercriticism, the ignorant enclosure, the bold brutality, the kind indecision, the placid delay, the magnanimous slackness.

sentences.zh: 必審己之所當戒，而齊之以義，然後中和之化應，而巧偽之徒不敢比周而望進。

translations.literal: One must examine what one ought to guard against in oneself and align it with righteousness—then the transformation of centrality and harmony responds, and the crafty and false dare not league together hoping for advancement.
translations.idiomatic: Know your own cautions, align them with right, and harmony answers while schemers lose hope of promotion.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下戒所以崇聖德。

translations.literal: Only let Your Majesty guard what is used to honor sagely virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Only see, my lord, what you must shun to crown sage virtue.

sentences.zh: 臣又聞室家之道修，則天下之理得，故《詩》始《國風》，《禮》本《冠》、《婚》。

translations.literal: Your subject has further heard that when the way of house and chamber is cultivated, then the pattern of all under heaven is obtained—therefore the Odes begins with the “Airs of the States,” and the Ritual roots in the capping and marriage rites.
translations.idiomatic: When the inner household is ordered, the empire is ordered—hence the Odes open with the Airs and the Ritual with capping and wedding.

sentences.zh: 始乎《國風》，原情性而明人倫也；

translations.literal: It begins with the “Airs of the States” to trace emotion and nature and clarify human relationships;
translations.idiomatic: The Airs begin by tracing feeling to clarify human ties;

sentences.zh: 本乎《冠》、《婚》，正基兆而防未然也。

translations.literal: it roots in capping and marriage to correct foundations and guard against what is not yet.
translations.idiomatic: capping and marriage root the rite in foundations and forestall future harm.

sentences.zh: 福之興莫不本乎室家。

translations.literal: The rise of blessing none fails to root in house and chamber.
translations.idiomatic: Every blessing flowers from the bedchamber.

sentences.zh: 道之衰莫不始乎閫內。

translations.literal: The decay of the way none fails to begin within the inner gates.
translations.idiomatic: Every ruin starts behind the women’s door.

sentences.zh: 故聖王必慎妃後之際，別適長之位。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage king must be careful at the juncture of consorts and queens and distinguish the places of heir and eldest.
translations.idiomatic: So the sage king weighs wives and heirs and marks the heir’s place.

sentences.zh: 禮之於內也。

translations.literal: These are the applications of ritual within the inner quarters.
translations.idiomatic: These are the rites that bind life within the women’s quarters.

sentences.zh: 卑不逾尊，新不先故，所以統人情而理陰氣也。

translations.literal: The low does not overstep the high, the new does not precede the old—by this one unifies human feelings and regulates yin qi.
translations.idiomatic: The humble must not leap the honored, nor the new outrank the old—thus feeling is unified and yin qi ordered.

sentences.zh: 其尊適而卑庶也，適子冠乎阼，禮之用醴，眾子不得與列，所以貴正體而明嫌疑也。

translations.literal: In honoring the heir and demeaning concubines, the heir’s son is capped on the east steps, the rite uses sweet wine, the many sons may not share the row—thereby to honor the true body and clarify suspicion.
translations.idiomatic: The true son is capped on the host’s steps, toasted with unfiltered ale, while lesser sons stand apart—honoring the legitimate body and sealing off doubt.

sentences.zh: 非虛加其禮文而已，乃中心與之殊異，故禮探其情而見之外也。

translations.literal: It is not vainly adding ornament to the ritual text; the center of the heart is thereby set apart from them differently—therefore ritual probes the feeling and displays it without.
translations.idiomatic: That is not empty show: the heart is truly divided, and ritual makes inner feeling visible without.

sentences.zh: 聖人動靜游燕，所親物得其序；

translations.literal: In movement and stillness, roaming and feasting, what the sage draws near obtains its proper order;
translations.idiomatic: Whether the sage moves or rests, feasts or travels, everything he draws near falls into its proper order.

sentences.zh: 得其序，則海內自修，百姓從化。

translations.literal: when order is obtained, then all within the seas cultivate themselves and the hundred surnames follow transformation.
translations.idiomatic: When order is set, the realm corrects itself and the people follow suit.

sentences.zh: 如當親者疏，當尊者卑，則佞巧之奸因時而動，以亂國家。

translations.literal: If what should be intimate is kept distant and what should be honored is made low, then the wickedness of flattery and craft seizes the moment to move and throw the state into chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Let kin grow cold and honor sink, and sycophants rush in to ruin the state.

sentences.zh: 故聖人慎防其端，禁於未然，不以私恩害公義。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage is careful to guard the beginning and forbid before it happens, not letting private kindness harm public duty.
translations.idiomatic: The sage blocks the first crack and never trades private grace for public right.

sentences.zh: 陛下聖德純備，莫不修正，則天下無為而治。

translations.literal: When Your Majesty’s sagely virtue is pure and complete, none fail to correct themselves—then all under heaven is governed without action.
translations.idiomatic: When your virtue is whole, every man straightens himself and the realm rules itself.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：「於以四方，克定厥家。」

translations.literal: The Odes says, “Thereby in the four directions, able to settle his house.”
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry says, “Thus in the four quarters he settled his house.”

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「正家而天下定矣。」

translations.literal: The tradition says, “Set the household in order and all under heaven is settled.”
translations.idiomatic: The tradition says, “Order the family and the world is fixed.”

sentences.zh: 衡為少傅數年，數上疏陳便宜，及朝廷有政議，傅經以對，言多法義。

translations.literal: Heng as junior tutor for several years several times submitted memorials stating what was advantageous, and whenever the court had policy debate he answered by expounding the classics—his words mostly matched law and principle.
translations.idiomatic: For years as junior tutor he filed practical advice and, whenever policy was debated, answered from the canon in terms of statute and right.

sentences.zh: 上以為任公卿，由是為光祿勳、御史大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor thought him fit for duke and minister rank; on this account he became Superintendent of the Imperial Household and Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged him fit for chancellorship and promoted him to superintendent of the household and imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 建昭三年，代韋玄成為丞相，封樂安侯，食邑六百戶。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianzhao he replaced Wei Xuancheng as chancellor, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Le’an, with six hundred households of revenue.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianzhao 3 he succeeded Wei Xuancheng as chancellor, enfeoffed as marquis of Le’an with six hundred taxable households.

sentences.zh: 元帝崩，成帝即位，衡上疏戒妃匹，勸經學威儀之則，曰：

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan died and Emperor Cheng took the throne, Heng submitted a memorial warning about consorts, urging the norms of classical learning and dignified bearing, saying:
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan died and Cheng mounted the throne, Heng warned him on wives and concubines and pressed the rules of scholarship and deportment:

sentences.zh: 陛下秉至考，哀傷思慕不絕於心，未有游虞弋射之宴，誠隆於慎終追遠，無窮已也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty embodies utmost filial piety; grief and longing for the dead never leave your heart; you have not yet had the feasts of roaming, hunting, or archery—truly you are lofty in carefully ending and long pursuing the distant, without end.
translations.idiomatic: Your filial grief for the late emperor knows no pause; you have not turned to hunt or feast—such care for the dead is profound indeed.

sentences.zh: 竊願陛下雖聖性得之，猶復加聖心焉。

translations.literal: Your subject privately wishes that although Your Majesty obtains this by sagely nature, you would still add sagely mind to it.
translations.idiomatic: Though this springs from your nature, add yet a sage’s deliberation.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「煢煢在疚」，言成王喪畢思慕，意氣未能平也，蓋所以就文、武之業，崇大化之本也。

translations.literal: The Odes says “alone in sorrow,” meaning that when King Cheng’s mourning ended his longing and longing, his spirit and temper had not yet settled—this is how he approached the work of Wen and Wu and honored the root of great transformation.
translations.idiomatic: The Odes speaks of “aching solitude”—King Cheng after the rites still mourned, his heart unquiet—by that he pressed on in Wen and Wu’s work and deepened the root of great peace.

sentences.zh: 臣又聞之師曰：「妃匹之際，生民之始，萬福之原。」

translations.literal: Your subject has further heard from the teacher: “At the juncture of consort matching lies the beginning of the living people, the source of myriad blessings.”
translations.idiomatic: My teacher said: “Marriage is where life begins and every blessing is born.”

sentences.zh: 婚姻之禮正，然後品物遂而天命全。

translations.literal: When the ritual of marriage is correct, then things take their grades and heaven’s mandate is complete.
translations.idiomatic: When wedding rite stands straight, things find their order and the mandate runs whole.

sentences.zh: 孔子論《詩》以《關睢》為始，言太上者民之父母，後夫人之行不侔乎天地，則無以奉神靈之統而理萬物之宜。

translations.literal: Confucius discussed the Odes taking “Guan ju” as the beginning, meaning that the highest above is parent to the people; if the conduct of empress and consorts does not match heaven and earth, then there is no means to uphold the line of spirits and gods and order the myriad things as is fitting.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius opened his discussion of the Odes with “Guan ju”: the sovereign must be father and mother to the people, and a consort’s conduct must mirror heaven and earth—else she cannot tend the ancestral cult or set the world aright.

sentences.zh: 故《詩》曰：「窈窕淑女，君子好仇。」

translations.literal: Therefore the Odes says, “The modest, retiring, virtuous, young lady—for the gentleman was she the fitting mate.”
translations.idiomatic: Hence the line: “That graceful maiden, fit mate for the gentleman.”

sentences.zh: 言能致其貞淑，不貳其操，情慾之感無介乎容儀，宴私之意不形乎動靜，夫然後可以配至尊而為宗廟主。

translations.literal: It means she can bring her chaste purity, not double her conduct, let no stir of passion come between her mien and bearing, nor private banquet intent show in movement or stillness—only then may she match the most honored and become mistress of the ancestral temple.
translations.idiomatic: She keeps faith and modesty, lets no lust touch her demeanor, hides private dalliance in deportment—only then may she wed the Son of Heaven and stand as mother of the shrines.

sentences.zh: 此綱紀之首，王教之端也。

translations.literal: This is the head of the net of law, the beginning of kingly teaching.
translations.idiomatic: That is the first cord of rule and the opening of royal instruction.

sentences.zh: 自上世已來，三代興廢，未有不由此者也。

translations.literal: From high antiquity onward, the rise and fall of the Three Ages none has failed to come through this.
translations.idiomatic: Since remote ages no rise or fall of the Three Dynasties has bypassed this truth.

sentences.zh: 願陛下詳覽得失盛衰之效以定大基，采有德，戒聲色，近嚴敬，遠技能。

translations.literal: I wish Your Majesty would carefully survey the effects of gain and loss, flourishing and decline, thereby to fix the great foundation, adopt the virtuous, guard against sound and color, draw near to stern reverence, and distance mere technical skill.
translations.idiomatic: Study how states thrive or fail, take the virtuous, shun pleasure, cleave to gravity, and spurn clever tricks—that will set the great foundation.

sentences.zh: 竊見聖德純茂，專精《詩》、《書》，好樂無厭。

translations.literal: Your subject has privately seen that sagely virtue is pure and rich, devoted solely to the Odes and Documents, with love of music never sated.
translations.idiomatic: Your virtue is deep and whole, your heart fixed on the Odes and Documents, your love of music insatiable.

sentences.zh: 臣衡材駑，無以輔相善義，宣揚德音。

translations.literal: Your subject Heng’s talent is a dull nag’s—he has no means to assist and complement good meanings or proclaim virtuous reputation.
translations.idiomatic: I am a poor nag, unfit to second your goodness or broadcast your fame.

sentences.zh: 臣聞《六經》者，聖人所以統天地之心，著善惡之歸，明吉凶之分，通人道之正，使不悖於其本性者也。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that the Six Classics are what the sages use to unify the mind of heaven and earth, set forth where good and evil return, clarify the division of auspicious and inauspicious, open the correctness of the human way, and keep men from betraying their inborn nature.
translations.idiomatic: The Six Classics are how the sages align heaven and man, trace good and evil, mark fortune from doom, and set the human path straight.

sentences.zh: 故審《六藝》之指，則天人之理可得而和，草木昆蟲可得而育，此永永不易之道也。

translations.literal: Therefore examine the pointers of the Six Arts, then the pattern of heaven and man may be obtained and harmonized, grass, trees, and insects may be obtained and nurtured—this is the way forever unchanging.
translations.idiomatic: Master the Six Arts and heaven answers man, life flourishes—that is the changeless way.

sentences.zh: 及《論語》、《孝經》，聖人言行之要，宜究其意。

translations.literal: As to the Analects and Classic of Filial Piety, they hold the essentials of the sages’ words and deeds; it is fitting to plumb their meaning.
translations.idiomatic: The Analects and Filial Piety hold the sage’s gist—sound them to the bottom.

sentences.zh: 臣又聞聖王之自為動靜周旋，奉天承親，臨朝享臣，物有節文，以章人倫。

translations.literal: Your subject has further heard that in the sage king’s own movements and stillness, turning and returning, serving heaven and receiving kin, facing court and feasting ministers, things have measured pattern-text to display human relationships.
translations.idiomatic: Every motion of the sage king—serving heaven, honoring kin, holding court, feasting ministers—follows patterned rite that displays the human order.

sentences.zh: 蓋欽翼祗栗，事天之容也；

translations.literal: To revere and assist, to be awed and trembling—this is the bearing toward heaven;
translations.idiomatic: Awe and trembling before heaven—that is the face turned toward the sky;

sentences.zh: 溫恭敬遜，承親之禮也；

translations.literal: warmth, respect, humility, yielding—this is the ritual toward kin;
translations.idiomatic: gentleness and yielding toward parents—that is the rite for kin;

sentences.zh: 正躬嚴恪，臨眾之儀也；

translations.literal: to correct the person and be stern and reverent—this is the deportment toward the multitude;
translations.idiomatic: straight spine and grave brow toward the crowd—that is the bearing for the multitude;

sentences.zh: 嘉惠和說，饗下之顏也。

translations.literal: kind favor and harmonious pleasure—this is the mien toward those below.
translations.idiomatic: kind looks and easy speech toward inferiors—that is the look for those below.

sentences.zh: 舉錯動作，物遵其儀，故形為仁義，動為法則。

translations.literal: In every raising or lowering, movement or act, things follow their pattern—therefore form becomes humaneness and righteousness, movement becomes model and rule.
translations.idiomatic: Every gesture follows its form, so stillness shows humaneness and right, motion becomes model for the realm.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「德義可尊，容止可觀，進退可度，以臨其民，是以其民畏而愛之，則而象之。」

translations.literal: Confucius said, “When virtue and righteousness may be honored, bearing and halt may be viewed, advance and retreat may be measured—thereby to face the people—then the people fear and love them, take them as model and image.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “When deportment wins honor and every step is measured, the people fear you, love you, and copy you.”

sentences.zh: 《大雅》云：「敬慎威儀，惟民之則。」

translations.literal: The Da Ya says, “Reverent and careful in awesome bearing—only thus are they a model for the people.”
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Ya says, “Reverent in every motion—they are the people’s pattern.”

sentences.zh: 諸侯正月朝覲天子，天子惟道德，昭穆穆以視之，又觀以禮樂，饗醴乃歸。

translations.literal: The feudal kings in the first month attend court to gaze on the Son of Heaven; the Son of Heaven, with virtue and power alone, shows them in solemn awe, then also displays ritual and music, feasts them with sweet wine, and they return.
translations.idiomatic: Each new year kings come to audience; the emperor receives them in solemn virtue, shows them ritual and music, feasts them, and sends them home.

sentences.zh: 故萬國莫不獲賜祉福，蒙化而成俗。

translations.literal: Therefore the myriad states none fail to receive the gift of blessing, are covered by transformation and complete custom.
translations.idiomatic: Thus every kingdom wins blessing and carries your transforming custom home.

sentences.zh: 今正月初幸路寢，臨朝賀，置酒以饗萬方，傳曰「君子慎始」，願陛下留神動靜之節，使群下得望盛德休光，以立基楨，天下幸甚！

translations.literal: Now on the first day of the first month you will for the first time visit the main hall, hold court congratulations, set out wine to feast the myriad directions—the tradition says “the gentleman is careful at the beginning”—I wish Your Majesty would fix attention on the measure of movement and stillness, so that those below may hope to see sagely virtue’s splendid light and thereby set foundation and frame—exceedingly fortunate for all under heaven!
translations.idiomatic: This new year’s first audience in the great hall, with wine for the realm, is the “careful beginning” the classics praise—mind every step of the rite so all may glimpse your light and the pillar of rule stand firm.

sentences.zh: 上敬納其言。

translations.literal: The emperor respectfully accepted his words.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took his advice to heart.

sentences.zh: 頃之，衡復奏正南北郊，罷諸淫祀，語在《郊祀志》。

translations.literal: Before long Heng again memorialized to correct the southern and northern suburban sacrifices and abolish the various licentious sacrifices—the account is in the “Treatise on Suburban Sacrifice.”
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he again urged fixing the suburban rites and cutting illicit shrines, as told in the Treatise on Suburban Sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 久之，衡子昌為越騎校尉，醉殺人，系詔獄。

translations.literal: After a long time Heng’s son Chang was Colonel of Swift Cavalry, got drunk and killed a man, and was bound in the edict prison.
translations.idiomatic: Years later his son Chang, colonel of swift cavalry, slew a man drunk and landed in the imperial jail.

sentences.zh: 越騎官屬與昌弟且謀篡昌。

translations.literal: Swift-cavalry subordinates plotted with Chang’s younger brother Dan to snatch Chang free.
translations.idiomatic: His staff conspired with Chang’s younger brother Dan to break him out.

sentences.zh: 事發覺，衡免冠徒跣待罪，天子使謁者詔衡冠履。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, Heng removed cap and shoes and waited for sentence; the Son of Heaven sent an usher to order Heng to cap and shoe himself.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot surfaced, Heng stripped cap and shoes to await punishment; the emperor sent an usher to tell him to dress again.

sentences.zh: 而有司奏衡專地盜土，衡竟坐免。

translations.literal: But the responsible officials memorialized that Heng had monopolized land and stolen territory on the map; Heng in the end sat guilty and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Officers then charged him with padding his fief map; he was dismissed.

sentences.zh: 子咸亦明經，歷位九卿。

translations.literal: His son Xian also was lucid in the classics and rose to the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: His son Xian too mastered the classics and reached the Nine Ministers.

sentences.zh: 家世多為博士者。

translations.literal: For generations the family produced many who became erudits.
translations.idiomatic: The house bred many court erudits.

sentences.zh: 張禹，字子文，河內軹人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Yu, courtesy name Ziwen, was a native of Zhi in Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Yu, style Ziwen, came from Zhi in Henei.

sentences.zh: 至禹父徙家蓮勺。

translations.literal: By Yu’s father’s time the family had moved to Lianzhuo.
translations.idiomatic: His father had moved the household to Lianzhuo.

sentences.zh: 禹為兒，數隨家至市，喜觀於卜相者前。

translations.literal: As a boy Yu several times followed the family to market and delighted to watch before the diviners and physiognomists.
translations.idiomatic: As a child he haunted the market stalls of diviners and face-readers.

sentences.zh: 久之，頗曉其別蓍布卦意，時從旁言。

translations.literal: After a long time he came largely to understand the meaning of sorting milfoil and spreading hexagrams, and from time to time spoke from the side.
translations.idiomatic: In time he grasped how to sort stalks and lay lines and would chip in from the crowd.

sentences.zh: 卜者愛之，又奇其面貌，謂禹父：「是兒多知，可令學經。」

translations.literal: The diviner loved him, also marveled at his features, and said to Yu’s father, “This boy knows much; you can have him study the classics.”
translations.idiomatic: The diviner doted on him, praised his face, and told his father, “The boy is quick—put him to the classics.”

sentences.zh: 及禹壯，至長安學，從沛郡施讎受《易》，琅邪王陽、膠東庸生問《論語》，既皆明習，有徒眾，舉為郡文學。

translations.literal: When Yu grew to adulthood he went to study in Chang’an, received the Changes from Shi Chou of Pei, and consulted Wang Yang of Langya and Yongsheng of Jiaodong on the Analects; when he had mastered them all and had a following of disciples, he was raised to literary scholar of the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Grown, he studied in Chang’an under Shi Chou for the Changes and with Wang Yang and Yongsheng for the Analects; he drew disciples and was named commandery literary scholar.

sentences.zh: 甘露中，諸儒薦禹，有詔太子太傅蕭望之問。

translations.literal: In the Ganlu period the various Confucians recommended Yu; there was an edict for Heir Apparent Grand Tutor Xiao Wangzhi to examine him.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Ganlu reign scholars recommended him, and Xiao Wangzhi was ordered to test him.

sentences.zh: 禹對《易》及《論語》大義，望之善焉，奏禹經學精習，有師法，可試事。

translations.literal: Yu answered on the great meanings of the Changes and Analects; Wangzhi thought well of him and memorialized that Yu’s classical learning was refined and practiced, followed a master’s method, and could be tried in office.
translations.idiomatic: His discourse on the Changes and Analects pleased Wangzhi, who reported him fit for trial office.

sentences.zh: 奏寢，罷歸故宮。

translations.literal: The memorial slept; he was dismissed and returned to his former post.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial stalled; he went home to his old post.

sentences.zh: 久之，試為博士。

translations.literal: After a long time he was tried out as erudit.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was appointed erudit on probation.

sentences.zh: 初元中，立皇太子，而博士鄭寬中以《尚書》授太子，薦言禹善說《論語》。

translations.literal: In the Chuyuan period the heir apparent was established, and Erudit Zheng Kuanzhong taught him the Documents; he recommended that Yu was good at expounding the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: When the crown prince was set in Chuyuan, Erudit Zheng Kuanzhong taught him the Documents and praised Yu’s Analects lectures.

sentences.zh: 詔令禹授太子《論語》，由是遷光祿大夫。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Yu to teach the heir apparent the Analects; on this account he was promoted to Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: The throne told Yu to instruct the heir in the Analects and made him palace grandee.

sentences.zh: 數歲，出為東平內史。

translations.literal: After several years he went out as Chancellor of the Interior to Dongping.
translations.idiomatic: A few years later he became inner chancellor to Dongping.

sentences.zh: 元帝崩，成帝即位，征禹、寬中，皆以師賜爵關內侯，寬中食邑八百戶，禹六百戶。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan died and Emperor Cheng took the throne, Yu and Kuanzhong were summoned; both were enfeoffed as full marquises within the passes for having been teachers—Kuanzhong with eight hundred households of revenue, Yu with six hundred.
translations.idiomatic: At Yuan’s death Cheng summoned both tutors and enfeoffed them as marquises within the passes—eight hundred households for Kuanzhong, six hundred for Yu.

sentences.zh: 拜為諸吏光祿大夫，秋中二千石，給事中，領尚書事。

translations.literal: He was appointed Palace Grandee among the various clerks, rank in autumn at two thousand piculs, attendant within the palace, directing the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: He took office as palace grandee of the first grade at two thousand piculs, palace attendant, with charge of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 是時，帝舅陽平侯王鳳為大將軍，輔政專權。

translations.literal: At this time the emperor’s uncle Marquis of Yangping Wang Feng was grand general, assisting government and monopolizing power.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor’s uncle Wang Feng, marquis of Yangping, was grand general and ruled the court.

sentences.zh: 而上富於春秋，謙讓，方鄉經學，敬重師傅。

translations.literal: The emperor was rich in spring and autumn, modest and yielding, was turning toward classical learning, and honored his teachers highly.
translations.idiomatic: The young emperor was humble, devout toward the classics, and reverent to his tutors.

sentences.zh: 而禹與鳳並領尚書，內不相安，數病，上書乞骸骨，欲退避鳳。

translations.literal: Yet Yu and Feng jointly directed the Masters of Writing; they were not harmonious within; Yu several times fell ill, submitted memorials begging leave on grounds of bones, wishing to retire and avoid Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Yu shared the secretariat with Feng, could not abide him, feigned illness, and begged retirement to escape Feng’s shadow.

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「朕以幼年執政，萬機懼失其中，君以道德為師，故委國政。

translations.literal: The emperor replied, “We took the government young and fear missing the mean in the myriad threads; you, sir, are Our teacher in virtue and power—therefore We entrust you with state affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng answered, “I took the throne young and dread every misstep; you are my moral teacher, so I laid state on you.

sentences.zh: 君何疑而數乞骸骨，忽忘雅素，欲避流言？

translations.literal: Why do you doubt and several times beg leave on bones, suddenly forgetting plain friendship, wishing to avoid floating rumor?
translations.idiomatic: Why doubt yourself, beg off again, forget old simplicity, and flee mere gossip?

sentences.zh: 朕無聞焉。

translations.literal: We have heard nothing.”
translations.idiomatic: I have heard nothing of the kind.”

sentences.zh: 君其固心致思，總秉諸事，推以孳孳，無違朕意。」

translations.literal: You, sir, should firm your mind and extend thought, overall grasp the various affairs, push on tirelessly, and do not disobey Our intention.”
translations.idiomatic: Steady your heart, think hard, hold every thread, work without slack, and do not leave my will.”

sentences.zh: 加賜黃金百斤、養牛、上尊酒，太官致餐，侍醫視疾，使者臨問。

translations.literal: In addition he bestowed one hundred jin of gold, a fattening ox, and fine wine, had the Grand Provisioner deliver meals, palace physicians watch his illness, and envoys attend him with inquiries.
translations.idiomatic: He added a hundred jin of gold, a fat ox, fine wine, imperial kitchens, physicians, and visiting envoys.

sentences.zh: 禹惶恐，復起視事，河平四年代王商為丞相，封安昌侯。

translations.literal: Yu was frightened, rose again to handle affairs, and in the fourth year of Heping replaced Wang Shang as chancellor, enfeoffed as Marquis of Anchang.
translations.idiomatic: Yu took fright, returned to duty, and in Heping 4 succeeded Wang Shang as chancellor, enfeoffed as marquis of Anchang.

sentences.zh: 為相六歲，鴻嘉元年以老病乞骸骨，上加優再三，乃聽許。

translations.literal: Having been chancellor six years, in the first year of Hongjia he begged leave on bones for old age and illness; the emperor added exceptional favor again and again, then permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: After six years as chancellor he retired ill in Hongjia 1; the emperor pressed rich favors on him thrice before consenting.

sentences.zh: 賜安車駟馬，黃金百斤，罷就第，以列侯朝朔望，位特進，見禮如丞相，置從事史五人，益封四百戶。

translations.literal: He was given a comfortable carriage and four horses, one hundred jin of gold, dismissed to his mansion, to attend court as a full marquis on new and full moons, rank specially advanced, received ritual like a chancellor, five clerk-advisors assigned him, and four hundred households added to his fief.
translations.idiomatic: He received a coach, four horses, a hundred jin of gold, kept marquis rank for the bimonthly audiences, title of specially advanced, rites equal to a chancellor, five staff officers, and four hundred extra households.

sentences.zh: 天子數加賞賜，前後數千萬。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven several times added rewards and gifts, front and back numbering in the tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor showered him with gifts worth tens of millions over the years.

sentences.zh: 禹為人謹厚，內殖貨財，家以田為業。

translations.literal: Yu as a man was careful and thick, inwardly he increased goods and wealth, and the family took fields as its occupation.
translations.idiomatic: He seemed cautious and stolid, yet fattened his purse and built the family on land.

sentences.zh: 及富貴，多買田至四百頃，皆涇、渭溉灌，極膏腴上賈。

translations.literal: When he became rich and noble he bought fields to four hundred qing, all irrigated by the Jing and Wei, of the richest soil at top price.
translations.idiomatic: Once powerful he bought four hundred qing of the fat Jing-Wei bottomland at top price.

sentences.zh: 它財物稱是。

translations.literal: His other goods and wealth matched this.
translations.idiomatic: His other holdings matched.

sentences.zh: 禹性習知音聲，內奢淫，身居大第，後堂理絲竹管弦。

translations.literal: Yu by nature was practiced in knowing music and sounds; inwardly he was extravagant and dissolute, dwelling in a great mansion while in the rear hall he set strings, winds, and pipes in order.
translations.idiomatic: He knew music by instinct, lived in luxury, and kept an orchestra in his rear hall.

sentences.zh: 禹成就弟子尤著者，淮陽彭宣至大司空，沛郡戴崇至少府九卿。

translations.literal: Among the disciples Yu brought to completion, the especially notable were Peng Xuan of Huaiyang, who reached Grand Minister of Works, and Dai Chong of Pei, who reached Privy Treasurer among the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: His star pupils included Peng Xuan of Huaiyang, who rose to grand minister of works, and Dai Chong of Pei, who reached privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 宣為人恭儉有法度，而崇愷弟多智，二人異行，禹心親愛崇，敬宣而疏之。

translations.literal: Xuan as a man was reverent, frugal, and had measure, while Chong was generous and brotherly and full of wit; the two had different conduct, yet in his heart Yu was intimate with Chong, respected Xuan but kept him at distance.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan was frugal and law-bound; Chong was genial and clever. Yu loved Chong and kept Xuan at arm’s length.

sentences.zh: 崇每候禹，常責師宜置酒設樂與弟子相娛。

translations.literal: Whenever Chong waited on Yu, he always pressed the teacher that he ought to set out wine and music to entertain disciples together.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Chong called, he nagged Yu to lay on wine and music for the disciples.

sentences.zh: 禹將崇入後堂飲食，婦女相對，優人管弦鏗鏘極樂，昏夜乃罷。

translations.literal: Yu would bring Chong into the rear hall for drink and food, women facing one another, actors and pipes clashing in utmost joy, only stopping at deep night.
translations.idiomatic: Yu took him to the inner hall, set women facing the guests, and ran musicians till midnight.

sentences.zh: 而宣之來也，禹見之於便坐，講論經義，日晏賜食，不過一肉卮酒相對。

translations.literal: When Xuan came, Yu received him in a side seat, discoursed on the classics’ meanings, at dusk bestowed food—not more than one meat and cup wine facing each other.
translations.idiomatic: When Xuan came he sat him in an antechamber, talked doctrine till dusk, and fed him a single dish and cup of wine.

sentences.zh: 宣未嘗得至後堂。

translations.literal: Xuan never attained entry to the rear hall.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan was never admitted to the inner hall.

sentences.zh: 及兩人皆聞知，各自得也。

translations.literal: When both men heard and knew, each got what suited him.
translations.idiomatic: Both understood the signal and took what came to them.

sentences.zh: 禹年老，自治塚塋，起祠室，好平陵肥牛亭部處地，又近延陵，奏請求之，上以賜禹，詔令平陵徙亭它所。

translations.literal: Yu in old age himself managed his tomb park, raised a shrine hall, loved the ground at the Fat Ox pavilion section of Pingling for its fertility and nearness to Yanling, memorialized requesting it; the emperor thereupon bestowed it on Yu and ordered Pingling to move the pavilion elsewhere.
translations.idiomatic: Aged, he laid out his tomb, fancied the Fat Ox pavilion tract near the imperial tombs, and petitioned for it; the emperor granted the land and moved the pavilion.

sentences.zh: 曲陽侯根聞而爭之：「此地當平陵寢廟衣冠所出遊道，禹為師傅，不遵謙讓，至求衣冠所游之道，又徙壞舊亭，重非所宜。

translations.literal: Marquis of Quyang Wang Gen heard and contested it: “This ground lies on the road by which the Pingling mausoleum temple’s caps and robes go forth on tour; Yu as teacher and tutor does not observe yielding—he has gone so far as to seek the road on which the caps and robes tour, and further moves and ruins the old pavilion—doubly what is not fitting.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Gen protested: “That strip is the spirit-way for Pingling’s temple regalia; for the emperor’s tutor to seize it and tear down an old post is doubly wrong.

sentences.zh: 孔子稱『賜愛其羊，我愛其禮』，宜更賜禹它地。」

translations.literal: Confucius praised “Zi’s love for the sheep—I love the ritual”; you should instead bestow other land on Yu.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius chose the rite over the sheep—give Yu some other plot.”

sentences.zh: 根雖為舅，上敬重之不如禹，根言雖切，猶不見從，卒以肥牛亭地賜禹。

translations.literal: Although Gen was maternal uncle, the emperor respected him less than Yu; though Gen’s words were cutting, still they were not followed, and in the end the Fat Ox pavilion land was bestowed on Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Gen was the emperor’s uncle yet less favored than Yu; despite his blunt protest the Fat Ox tract went to Yu.

sentences.zh: 根由是害禹寵，數毀惡之。

translations.literal: Gen therefore resented Yu’s favor and several times slandered him.
translations.idiomatic: Gen nursed jealousy and slandered him often.

sentences.zh: 天子愈益敬厚禹。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven all the more respected and treated Yu thickly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor only grew more deferential.

sentences.zh: 禹每病，輒以起居聞，車駕自臨問之。

translations.literal: Whenever Yu was ill, the emperor always had his daily routine reported, and the chariot in person came to inquire.
translations.idiomatic: Each illness brought daily bulletins on his health and a personal visit from the throne.

sentences.zh: 上親拜禹床下，禹頓首謝恩，因歸誠，言：「老臣有四男一女，愛女其於男，遠嫁為張掖太守蕭咸妻，不勝父子私情，思與相近。」

translations.literal: The emperor in person bowed below Yu’s couch; Yu knocked his head in thanks for favor and, yielding sincerity, said, “The old minister has four sons and one daughter; he loves the daughter more than the sons; she has married far as wife to Zhangye Governor Xiao Xian, and I cannot overcome the private feeling of father and child—I wish to be near her.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor bowed at his bedside; Yu kowtowed and confessed he doted on a daughter married far to Zhangye governor Xiao Xian and longed to have her near.

sentences.zh: 上即時徙咸為弘農太守。

translations.literal: The emperor immediately moved Xian to be Governor of Hongnong.
translations.idiomatic: The throne at once transferred Xian to Hongnong, closer to the capital.

sentences.zh: 又禹小子未有宮，上臨候禹，禹數視其小子，上即禹床下拜為黃門郎，給事中。

translations.literal: Moreover Yu’s youngest son had not yet an office; when the emperor came to wait on Yu, Yu several times looked at his youngest son; the emperor thereupon below Yu’s couch invested him as Gentleman of the Yellow Gates, attendant within the palace.
translations.idiomatic: His youngest son lacked rank; during a sickbed visit Yu kept glancing at the boy until the emperor made him a gentleman of the yellow gates on the spot.

sentences.zh: 禹雖家居，以特進為天子師，國家每有大政，必與定議。

translations.literal: Although Yu dwelt at home, as specially advanced he was the Son of Heaven’s teacher; whenever the state had great policy, he was sure to join in fixing the decision.
translations.idiomatic: Though retired at specially advanced rank he remained imperial tutor and was consulted on every great decision.

sentences.zh: 永始、元延之間，日蝕、地震尤數，吏民多上書言災異之應，譏切王氏專政所致。

translations.literal: Between Yongshi and Yuanyan, solar eclipses and earthquakes were especially frequent; clerks and commoners in numbers submitted memorials stating the response of disasters and anomalies, cuttingly blaming the Wang clan’s monopoly of government.
translations.idiomatic: From Yongshi to Yuanyan eclipses and earthquakes multiplied; memorials blamed the Wangs’ monopoly of power.

sentences.zh: 上懼變異數見，意頗然之，而未有以明見，乃車駕至禹弟，辟左右，親問禹以天變，因用吏民所言王氏事示禹。

translations.literal: The emperor feared that changes and anomalies appeared repeatedly, his mind largely agreed, yet had no clear way to see it; then he drove his chariot to Yu’s mansion, dismissed those at his sides, and personally asked Yu about heaven’s changes, thereby showing Yu what clerks and commoners had said about the Wang affair.
translations.idiomatic: Frightened by the portents yet unsure how to act, he drove alone to Yu’s house, sent attendants away, asked about omens, and showed him the anti-Wang petitions.

sentences.zh: 禹自見年老，子孫弱，又與曲陽侯不平，恐為所怨。

translations.literal: Yu, seeing himself old, sons and grandsons weak, and also not at peace with Marquis of Quyang, feared he would incur their resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Yu saw his age, his weak heirs, and his feud with Wang Gen and feared revenge.

sentences.zh: 禹則謂上曰：「春秋二百四十二年間，日蝕三十餘，地震五，或為諸侯自殺，或夷狄侵中國，災變之異深遠難見，故聖人罕言命，不語怪神。

translations.literal: Yu then said to the emperor, “In the two hundred forty-two years of the Spring and Autumn, there were more than thirty solar eclipses and five earthquakes—sometimes feudal lords killed one another, sometimes the Yi and Di invaded the Central States; the strangeness of disaster and anomaly is deep and far, hard to see—therefore the sages seldom spoke of fate and did not discuss prodigies and spirits.
translations.idiomatic: He answered, “In 242 years the Annals record thirty-odd eclipses and five quakes—some from feuding lords, some from barbarian raids; omens are obscure, so the sages rarely spoke of fate or ghosts.

sentences.zh: 性與天道，自子贛之屬不得聞，何況淺見鄙儒之所言！

translations.literal: Nature and the way of heaven—even men of Zigong’s sort could not hear of them—how much less what shallow-viewed vulgar Confucians say!
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s nature was hidden even from Zigong—why trust shallow Ru gossip?

sentences.zh: 陛下宜修政事以善應之，與下同其福喜，此經義意也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty should cultivate government affairs to respond well, share blessing and joy with those below—this is the meaning of the classics’ righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Set policy right and share good fortune with the people—that is the classic teaching.

sentences.zh: 新學小生，亂道誤人，宜無信用，以經術斷之。」

translations.literal: New-learning young sprigs confuse the way and mislead men—they should not be trusted; judge them by classical technique.”
translations.idiomatic: Neophytes who twist the way deserve no hearing—rule them by the canon.”

sentences.zh: 上雅信愛禹，曲此不疑王氏。

translations.literal: The emperor by habit trusted and loved Yu; on this account he did not doubt the Wangs.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng trusted Yu implicitly and let the Wang matter drop.

sentences.zh: 後曲陽侯根及諸王子弟聞知禹言，皆喜說，遂親就禹。

translations.literal: Later when Marquis of Quyang Gen and the various Wang princely sons and younger brothers heard and knew Yu’s words, they were all pleased and drew near to Yu.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Gen and his kin heard Yu had shielded them, they flocked to him with gratitude.

sentences.zh: 禹見時有變異，若上體不安，常擇日潔齋露蓍，正衣冠立筮，得吉卦則獻其占，如有不吉，禹為感動有憂色。

translations.literal: Whenever Yu saw there were changes and anomalies, or if the emperor’s person was unwell, he always chose a day, purified and fasted, exposed the milfoil, straightened cap and gown, and stood to divine; if he obtained an auspicious hexagram he presented its divination; if it was not auspicious, Yu for his part was moved and showed a troubled countenance.
translations.idiomatic: When omens appeared or the emperor ailed, he fasted, laid out the stalks in full dress, and presented only lucky readings—or looked stricken if the lines were bad.

sentences.zh: 初，禹為師，以上難數對己問經，為《論語章句》獻之。

translations.literal: At the beginning, when Yu was teacher, because the emperor had difficulty repeatedly putting classical questions to him, he composed “Chapter and Sentence on the Analects” and presented it.
translations.idiomatic: Early in his tutorship, wearied by the emperor’s constant questions, he compiled a chapter-and-sentence Analects and presented it.

sentences.zh: 始，魯扶卿及夏侯勝、王陽、蕭望之、韋玄成皆說《論語》，篇第或異。

translations.literal: Originally Fu Qing of Lu and Xiahou Sheng, Wang Yang, Xiao Wangzhi, and Wei Xuancheng all lectured on the Analects, but the order of sections sometimes differed.
translations.idiomatic: Lu’s Fu Qing, Xiahou Sheng, Wang Yang, Xiao Wangzhi, and Wei Xuancheng had all lectured the Analects with differing chapter orders.

sentences.zh: 禹先事王陽，後從庸生，采獲所安，最後出而尊貴。

translations.literal: Yu first studied under Wang Yang, afterward followed Yongsheng, gathered what he found sound, and last came forth honored and exalted.
translations.idiomatic: Yu began with Wang Yang, then Yongsheng, picked the best of each, and last won highest place.

sentences.zh: 諸儒為之語曰：「欲為《論》，念張文。」

translations.literal: The scholars made a saying about him: “If you wish to work on the Lun, think on Zhang Wen.”
translations.idiomatic: Scholars rhymed, “For the Lun, think Zhang Wen.”

sentences.zh: 由是學者多從張氏，余家寢微。

translations.literal: Therefore students mostly followed the Zhang school, and the other houses gradually declined.
translations.idiomatic: Disciples flocked to Zhang’s reading while rival schools faded.

sentences.zh: 孔光，字子夏，孔子十四世之孫也。

translations.literal: Kong Guang, courtesy name Zixia, was the fourteenth-generation descendant of Confucius.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Guang, style Zixia, was Confucius’s fourteenth-generation heir.

sentences.zh: 孔子生伯魚鯉，鯉生子思伋，伋生子上帛，帛生子家求，求生子真箕，箕生子高穿。

translations.literal: Confucius bore Bo Yu, named Li; Li bore Zisi, named Ji; Ji bore Shangzi, named Bo; Bo bore Zijia, named Qiu; Qiu bore Zizhen, named Jin; Jin bore Zigao, named Chuan.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius’s line runs Bo Yu (Kong Li), then Zisi (Kong Ji), then Shangzi (Kong Bo), Zijia (Kong Qiu), Zizhen (Kong Jin), and Zigao (Kong Chuan).

sentences.zh: 穿生順，順為魏相。

translations.literal: Chuan bore Shun; Shun was Wei chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Chuan sired Shun, who served as chancellor of Wei.

sentences.zh: 順生鮒，鮒為陳涉博士，死陳下。

translations.literal: Shun bore Fu; Fu was Chen She’s erudit and died below Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Shun sired Fu, erudit to Chen She, who died beneath Chen.

sentences.zh: 鮒弟子襄為孝惠博士、長沙太博。

translations.literal: Fu’s disciple Xiang was erudit under Emperor Hui and grand tutor to Changsha.
translations.idiomatic: Fu’s pupil Xiang was Hui’s erudit and grand tutor in Changsha.

sentences.zh: 襄生忠，忠生武及安國，武生延年。

translations.literal: Xiang bore Zhong; Zhong bore Wu and Anguo; Wu bore Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang sired Zhong; Zhong sired Wu and Anguo; Wu sired Yannian.

sentences.zh: 延年生霸，字次儒。

translations.literal: Yannian bore Ba, courtesy name Ciru.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian’s son was Ba, styled Ciru.

sentences.zh: 霸生光焉。

translations.literal: Ba bore Guang thereupon.
translations.idiomatic: Ba sired Guang.

sentences.zh: 安國、延年皆以治《尚書》為武帝博士。

translations.literal: Anguo and Yannian both took the Documents as their field of study and became erudits to Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Anguo and Yannian both mastered the Documents and served Wu as court erudits.

sentences.zh: 安國至臨淮太守。

translations.literal: Anguo reached Governor of Linhuai.
translations.idiomatic: Anguo rose to governor of Linhuai.

sentences.zh: 霸亦治《尚書》，事太傅夏侯勝，昭帝末年為博士，宣帝時為太中大夫，以選授皇太子經，遷詹事、高密相。

translations.literal: Ba also studied the Documents, served Grand Tutor Xiahou Sheng, at the end of Emperor Zhao’s reign became erudit, under Emperor Xuan became Grand Counsellor of the Palace, by selection was appointed to teach the heir apparent the classics, and was promoted to Superintendent of the Household and Chancellor of Gaomi.
translations.idiomatic: Ba too studied the Documents under Grand Tutor Xiahou Sheng, became erudit late in Zhao’s reign, palace grandee under Xuan, then tutor to the crown prince, and rose to superintendent of the household and chancellor of Gaomi.

sentences.zh: 是時，諸侯王相在郡守上。

translations.literal: At this time chancellors to feudal kings ranked above commandery governors.
translations.idiomatic: In that era a king’s chancellor outranked a commandery governor.

sentences.zh: 綏和中，上即位二十五年，無繼嗣，至親有同產弟中山孝王及同產弟子定陶王在。

translations.literal: In the Suhe period the emperor had been on the throne twenty-five years without an heir; among closest kin there were his full younger brother Prince Xiao of Zhongshan and his full nephew’s son the King of Dingtao.
translations.idiomatic: Under Suhe the emperor had reigned twenty-five years without an heir; his nearest kin were his uterine brother, Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, and his nephew’s son, the king of Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 定陶王好學多材，子帝子行。

translations.literal: The King of Dingtao loved learning and had many talents; his conduct matched that of an imperial son.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Dingtao was learned, able, and carried himself like a true son of the house.

sentences.zh: 而王祖母傅太后陰為王求漢嗣，私事趙皇后、昭儀及帝舅大司馬驃騎將軍王根，故皆勸上。

translations.literal: But the king’s grandmother Grand Empress Dowager Fu secretly sought the Han succession for the king, privately cultivating Empress Zhao, the Brilliant Companion, and the emperor’s uncle Grand Marshal and General of Agile Cavalry Wang Gen—therefore all urged the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: His grandmother Lady Fu worked behind the throne for him, courting Empress Zhao, Lady Zhao the brilliant companion, and the emperor’s uncle Wang Gen—so all pressed the choice.

sentences.zh: 上於是召丞相翟方進、御史大夫光、右將軍廉褒、後將軍硃博，皆引入禁中，議中山、定陶王誰宜為嗣者。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon summoned Chancellor Zhai Fangjin, Imperial Counselor Guang, General of the Right Lian Bao, and General of the Rear Zhu Bo, all led within the forbidden precincts, to deliberate which of the Kings of Zhongshan and Dingtao was fit to be heir.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng summoned Zhai Fangjin, Kong Guang, Lian Bao, and Zhu Bo into the palace to debate which prince should succeed.

sentences.zh: 方進、根以為：「定陶王帝弟之子，《禮》曰：『昆弟之子猶子也』，『為其後者為之子也』，定陶王宜為嗣。」

translations.literal: Fangjin and Gen thought: “The King of Dingtao is the son of the emperor’s younger brother; the Ritual says, ‘A younger brother’s son is like a son,’ ‘One who continues his line is his son’—the King of Dingtao should be heir.”
translations.idiomatic: Fangjin and Wang Gen cited the Ritual: a brother’s son counts as one’s own; the heir should be the king of Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 褒、傅皆如方進、根議。

translations.literal: Bao and Bo both agreed with Fangjin and Gen’s opinion.
translations.idiomatic: Bao and Bo concurred.

sentences.zh: 光獨以為禮立嗣以親，中山王先帝之子，帝親弟也，以《尚書‧盤庚》殷之及王為比，中山王宜為嗣。

translations.literal: Guang alone thought that by ritual establishing an heir follows closeness of kin: the King of Zhongshan was the former emperor’s son and the emperor’s full younger brother; comparing to Yin’s “brother succeeds brother” kings in the “Pan Geng” section of the Documents, the King of Zhongshan should be heir.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Guang alone argued for kinship order: Zhongshan was the late emperor’s son and Cheng’s full brother—like Yin’s brother succession in “Pan Geng”—so Zhongshan should inherit.

sentences.zh: 上以《禮》兄弟不相入廟，又皇后、昭儀欲立定陶王，故遂立為太子。

translations.literal: The emperor, because the Ritual says brothers do not enter one another’s temples, and also because the empress and brilliant companion wished to establish the King of Dingtao, therefore finally established him as heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng held that brothers could not share one temple line; the empress and Lady Zhao wanted Dingtao—so Dingtao became crown prince.

sentences.zh: 光以議不中意，左遷廷尉。

translations.literal: Guang because his opinion did not hit the mark was demoted left to commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Guang’s dissent earned him a demotion to commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 光久典尚書，練法令，號稱詳平。

translations.literal: Guang long had charge of the Masters of Writing, was practiced in statutes and ordinances, and was famed as thorough and even-handed.
translations.idiomatic: Long at the secretariat, he knew law inside out and was praised as fair.

sentences.zh: 時定陵侯淳于長坐大逆誅，長小妻迺始等六人皆以長事未發覺時棄去，或更嫁。

translations.literal: At this time Marquis of Dingling Chunyu Chang sat guilty of great rebellion and was executed; six of Chang’s junior wives such as Naishi had all left Chang before the affair was discovered, when the matter had not yet come to light, some remarrying.
translations.idiomatic: When Chunyu Chang of Dingling died for treason, six junior wives including Naishi had already left him—some remarried—before his plot surfaced.

sentences.zh: 用長事發，丞相方進，大司空武議，以為：「令，犯法者各以法時律令論之，明有所訖也，長犯大逆時，迺始等見為長妻，已有當坐之罪，與身犯法無異。

translations.literal: When Chang’s affair came to light, Chancellor Fangjin and Grand Minister of Works Wu deliberated and thought: “By ordinance, each offender is judged by the statutes and ordinances in force at the time of the offense, making clear where judgment ends; when Chang committed great rebellion, Naishi and the others were regarded as Chang’s wives and already bore guilt that should sit with them, no different from personally violating law.
translations.idiomatic: Fangjin and Grand Minister of Works Wu held that law must judge by the code in force at the crime; when Chang rebelled, Naishi and the others were his wives and already liable as if they had joined the crime.

sentences.zh: 後乃棄去，於法無以解。

translations.literal: Afterward they abandoned him and left—by law there is no way to excuse that.
translations.idiomatic: Leaving him later cannot erase that liability.

sentences.zh: 請論。」

translations.literal: We request judgment.”
translations.idiomatic: They asked for conviction.”

sentences.zh: 光議以為：「大逆無道，父母妻子同產無少長皆棄市，欲懲後犯法者也。

translations.literal: Guang deliberated and thought: “Great rebellion and lack of the Way—parents, wives, children, and full kin without distinction of age are all exposed in the market, intending to warn later offenders.
translations.idiomatic: Guang countered: treason exposes whole families in the market to warn the world.

sentences.zh: 夫婦之道，有義則合，無義則離。

translations.literal: The way of husband and wife: where there is duty they unite; where duty ends they part.
translations.idiomatic: Marriage binds only while duty holds; when duty ends, spouses part.

sentences.zh: 長未自知當坐大逆之法，而棄去迺始等，或更嫁，義已絕，而欲以為長妻論殺之，名不正，不當坐。」

translations.literal: Chang did not yet know he would sit under the law of great rebellion when he cast off Naishi and the others, or some remarried—the duty was already severed; yet to treat them as Chang’s wives and discuss killing them—the name is not correct; they should not sit guilty.”
translations.idiomatic: Chang had not yet been condemned when those women left or remarried—the tie was dead; to execute them as traitors’ wives would misapply the law.

sentences.zh: 有詔「光議是」。

translations.literal: There was an edict: “Guang’s opinion is correct.”
translations.idiomatic: An edict upheld Guang’s view.

sentences.zh: 是歲，右將軍褒、後將軍博坐定陵、紅陽侯皆免為庶人。

translations.literal: That year Generals of the Right Bao and Rear Bo, sitting guilt with the Marquises of Dingling and Hongyang, were both stripped to commoners.
translations.idiomatic: The same year Lian Bao and Zhu Bo were degraded with the marquises of Dingling and Hongyang.

sentences.zh: 以光為左將軍，居右將軍官職，執金吾王咸為右將軍，居後將軍官職。

translations.literal: Guang was made General of the Left, holding the former General of the Right’s office; Bearer of the Mace Wang Xian was made General of the Right, holding the former General of the Rear’s office.
translations.idiomatic: Guang became general of the left in Bao’s old post; Wang Xian became general of the right in Bo’s place.

sentences.zh: 罷後將軍官。

translations.literal: The post of General of the Rear was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The rear general’s billet was left empty.

sentences.zh: 數月，丞相方進薨，召左將軍光，當拜，已刻侯印書贊，上暴崩，即其夜於大行前拜受丞相、博山侯印綬。

translations.literal: Several months later Chancellor Fangjin died; the left general Guang was summoned for investiture—the marquis seal was already carved and the encomium written—when the emperor suddenly died; that very night before the late emperor’s bier he received investiture as chancellor and Marquis of Boshan.
translations.idiomatic: Months later Fangjin died; Guang was called to be chancellor, the seal cut and patent drafted—then Cheng died suddenly, and that night Guang took the chancellor’s seal and Boshan marquisate before the imperial catafalque.

sentences.zh: 哀帝初即位，躬行儉約，省減諸用，政事由己出，朝廷翕然，望至治焉。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai first took the throne, he personally practiced frugality, cut and reduced the various expenditures, government affairs issued from himself, and the court was of one breath, hoping for utmost good order.
translations.idiomatic: Ai began his reign frugal, hands-on, and austere; the court briefly hoped for order.

sentences.zh: 褒賞大臣，益封光千戶。

translations.literal: He rewarded great ministers and added one thousand households to Guang’s fief.
translations.idiomatic: He enlarged Guang’s fief by a thousand households.

sentences.zh: 時，成帝母太皇太后自居長樂宮，而帝祖母定陶傅太后在國邸，有詔問丞相、大司空：「定陶共王太后宜當何居？」
