translations.literal: At this time Emperor Cheng’s mother the Grand Empress Dowager herself dwelt in Changle Palace, while the emperor’s grandmother Grand Empress Dowager Fu of Dingtao was in the princely lodge in the capital; there was an edict asking the chancellor and grand minister of works: “Where should the Grand Empress Dowager of Prince Gong of Dingtao dwell?”
translations.idiomatic: Cheng’s mother held Changle; Ai’s grandmother Lady Fu lodged in the princely residence; the edict asked where Lady Fu should live.

sentences.zh: 光素聞傅太后為人剛暴，長於權謀，自帝在襁褓而養長教道至於成人，帝之立又有力。

translations.literal: Guang had long heard that Grand Empress Dowager Fu as a person was hard and violent, skilled in power stratagems, had from the emperor in swaddling clothes reared and taught him to adulthood, and when the emperor was established had also been forceful.
translations.idiomatic: Guang knew Lady Fu as fierce, shrewd, Ai’s nurse and tutor from infancy, and a power behind his enthronement.

sentences.zh: 光心恐傅太后與政事，不欲令與帝旦夕相近，即議以為定陶太后宜改築宮。

translations.literal: Guang in his heart feared that Grand Empress Dowager Fu would join in government affairs and did not wish to let her be near the emperor morning and evening; he immediately deliberated that the Dingtao grand empress dowager ought to have a palace built anew.
translations.idiomatic: He feared she would rule through Ai and urged building her a separate palace to keep her from the inner court.

sentences.zh: 大司空何武曰：「可居北宮。」

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Works He Wu said, “She may dwell in the Northern Palace.”
translations.idiomatic: He Wu said she could move to the Northern Palace.

sentences.zh: 上從武言。

translations.literal: The emperor followed Wu’s words.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 北宮有紫房復道通未央宮，傅太后果從復道朝夕至帝所，求欲稱尊號，貴寵其親屬，使上不得直道行。

translations.literal: The Northern Palace had purple chambers and a covered passage communicating with Weiyang Palace; Grand Empress Dowager Fu thereupon used the covered passage morning and evening to reach the emperor’s quarters, sought to take a honored title, ennobled and favored her kin, and kept the emperor from walking the straight path.
translations.idiomatic: But Northern Palace linked to Weiyang by a covered walk; Lady Fu used it daily, pressed for titles, packed court with her kin, and bent the young emperor.

sentences.zh: 頃之，太后從弟子傅遷在左右尤傾邪，上免官遣歸故郡。

translations.literal: Before long the empress dowager’s sister’s son Fu Qian, attending at the emperor’s side, was especially crooked; the emperor dismissed him from office and sent him back to his home commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Soon her nephew Fu Qian, a favorite attendant, proved vicious; Ai dismissed him to his home commandery.

sentences.zh: 傅太后怒，上不得已復留遷。

translations.literal: Grand Empress Dowager Fu was angry; the emperor had no choice but to retain Qian again.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Fu raged until Ai took Qian back.

sentences.zh: 光與大司空師丹奏言：「詔書『侍中、駙馬都尉遷巧佞無義，漏洩不忠，國之賊也，免歸故郡。』

translations.literal: Guang and Grand Minister of Works Shi Dan memorialized: “The edict said, ‘Palace Attendant and Chief of Palace Gentlemen for the Imperial Grooms Qian is crafty and flattering, without duty, leaks secrets and is disloyal—a thief of the state; dismiss him to his home commandery.’
translations.idiomatic: Guang and Shi Dan cited the edict cashiering Fu Qian as attendant and chief of mounted escort for treachery.

sentences.zh: 復有詔止。

translations.literal: Again there was an edict stopping it.
translations.idiomatic: A counter-edict stayed his banishment.

sentences.zh: 天下疑惑，無所取信，虧損聖德，誠不小愆。

translations.literal: All under heaven was doubtful and had nothing to trust in—damage to sagely virtue is truly no small fault.
translations.idiomatic: The realm lost faith in the throne—a real wound to imperial virtue.

sentences.zh: 陛下以變異連見，避正殿，見群臣，思求其故，至今未有所改。

translations.literal: Your Majesty because changes and anomalies have appeared in succession has avoided the main hall, met the ministers, and sought the reason—yet to this day nothing has been reformed.
translations.idiomatic: You moved court to ponder the omens, yet nothing changed.

sentences.zh: 臣請歸遷故郡，以銷奸黨，應天戒。」

translations.literal: Your subjects request returning Qian to his home commandery to dissolve the wicked faction and answer heaven’s warning.”
translations.idiomatic: We beg you send Qian away again to break this cabal and answer heaven.”

sentences.zh: 卒不得遣，復為侍中。

translations.literal: In the end he could not be sent away and again became palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Qian was never banished and kept his post.

sentences.zh: 脅於傅太后，皆此類也。

translations.literal: Coerced by Grand Empress Dowager Fu—matters were all of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: Such was Ai’s life under Lady Fu’s thumb.

sentences.zh: 又傅太后欲與成帝母俱稱尊號，群下多順詣，言母以子貴，宜立尊號以厚孝道。

translations.literal: Moreover Grand Empress Dowager Fu wished to share honored titles with Emperor Cheng’s mother; those below mostly bent to her will, saying the mother is honored by the son and it was fitting to establish honored titles to thicken filial duty.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Fu also demanded equal title with Cheng’s mother; courtiers flattered her with “the mother rises with the son.”

sentences.zh: 唯師丹與光持不可。

translations.literal: Only Shi Dan and Guang held that it could not be done.
translations.idiomatic: Only Shi Dan and Kong Guang refused.

sentences.zh: 上重違大臣正議，又內迫傅太后，猗違者連歲。

translations.literal: The emperor heavily could not go against the ministers’ straight opinion, yet was inwardly pressed by Grand Empress Dowager Fu, and wavered for successive years.
translations.idiomatic: Ai hated to override his ministers yet could not refuse Lady Fu, and wavered for years.

sentences.zh: 丹以罪免，而硃博代為大司空。

translations.literal: Dan was removed for a crime, and Zhu Bo replaced him as grand minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: Dan fell; Zhu Bo took his place as grand minister of works.

sentences.zh: 光自先帝時議繼嗣有持異之隙矣，又重忤傅太后指，由是傅氏在位者與硃博為表裡，共毀譖光。

translations.literal: Guang from the former emperor’s time in deliberating the heir had had a crack of holding a different view, and again had gravely disobeyed Grand Empress Dowager Fu’s intention—therefore those of the Fu clan in office league with Zhu Bo on the surface and within, jointly slandering Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Guang had crossed the Fu clan at the succession and again defied Lady Fu; her kin and Zhu Bo slandered him in concert.

sentences.zh: 後數月遂策免光曰：「丞相者，朕之股肱，所與共承宗廟，統理海內，輔朕之不逮以治天下也。

translations.literal: Several months later they finally issued a rescript dismissing Guang, saying: “The chancellor is Our forearm and thigh, with whom We jointly uphold the ancestral temple and order all within the seas, aiding Our lack to govern all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Months later an edict dismissed him: “The chancellor is my right arm, co-heir of the shrines, orderer of the realm, my crutch in rule.

sentences.zh: 朕既不明，災異重仍，日月無光，山崩河決，五星失行，是章朕之不德而股肱之不良也。

translations.literal: We are already not enlightened; disasters and anomalies pile up again and again, sun and moon without light, mountains collapse and rivers burst, the five planets lose their courses—this displays Our lack of virtue and the badness of Our forearm and thigh.
translations.idiomatic: I am dim; omens pile up—eclipses, quakes, floods, wandering stars—showing my fault and my minister’s failure.

sentences.zh: 君前為御史大夫，輔翼先帝，出入八年，卒無忠言嘉謀；

translations.literal: You, sir, formerly as imperial counselor aided the former emperor, in and out eight years, and in the end had no loyal words or fine plans;
translations.idiomatic: For eight years as imperial counselor you aided the late emperor yet never offered loyal counsel or a sound plan.

sentences.zh: 今相朕，出入三年，憂國之風復無聞焉。

translations.literal: now assisting Us, in and out three years, We again hear nothing of a wind of worrying for the state.
translations.idiomatic: For three years as chancellor you have shown no care for the state.

sentences.zh: 陰陽錯謬，歲比不登，天下空虛，百姓饑饉，父子分散，流離道路，以十萬數。

translations.literal: Yin and yang are awry, year after year the harvest does not ripen, all under heaven is empty, the hundred surnames suffer famine, fathers and sons scatter, drift and wander on the roads, numbering in the hundreds of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Yin and yang are wrong; harvests fail; the people starve and scatter by the hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 而百官群職曠廢，奸軌放縱，盜賊並起，或攻官寺，殺長吏。

translations.literal: Yet the hundred offices and group duties lie idle and abandoned, wicked tracks run free, bandits rise together, some attacking official compounds and killing senior clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Offices rot, crime spreads, rebels storm yamens and kill magistrates.

sentences.zh: 數以問君，君無怵惕憂懼之意，對毋能為。

translations.literal: Several times We have asked you, sir; you have had no look of fear or dread, and in reply could do nothing.
translations.idiomatic: I have asked you again and again; you show no alarm and have no remedy.

sentences.zh: 是以群卿大夫咸惰哉莫以為意，咎由君焉。

translations.literal: Therefore the assembled ministers and grandees all grow lazy—none takes it to heart—the fault lies with you, sir.”
translations.idiomatic: So every minister grows slack—the blame is yours.”

sentences.zh: 君秉社稷之重，總百僚之任，上無以匡朕之闕，下不能綏安百姓。

translations.literal: You, sir, hold the weight of soil and grain and overall the burden of the hundred offices; above you have no means to correct Our faults, below you cannot pacify and secure the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: You hold the altars yet mend neither my faults nor the people’s woes.

sentences.zh: 《書》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the Documents say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Documents say?

sentences.zh: 『毋曠庶官，天工人其代之』。

translations.literal: ‘Do not leave the multitude of offices empty—heaven’s work, men substitute for it.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Let no office stand empty—men stand in for heaven’s work.’

sentences.zh: 於虖！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 君其上丞相、博山侯印綬，罷歸。」

translations.literal: You, sir, shall present the chancellor’s and Marquis of Boshan seals and ribbons and be dismissed to return home.”
translations.idiomatic: Surrender the chancellor’s seal and the marquisate of Boshan and go home.”

sentences.zh: 光退閭里，杜門自守。

translations.literal: Guang retired to his lane, closed his gate, and guarded himself.
translations.idiomatic: Guang shut his gate and lived in seclusion.

sentences.zh: 而硃博代為丞相，數月，坐承傅太后指妄奏事自殺。

translations.literal: But Zhu Bo replaced him as chancellor; several months later, sitting guilt for falsely memorializing on affairs under Grand Empress Dowager Fu’s instruction, he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Bo succeeded him, then killed himself for forging policy at Lady Fu’s dictation.

sentences.zh: 平當代為丞相，數月薨。

translations.literal: Ping Dang replaced him as chancellor and died several months later.
translations.idiomatic: Ping Dang lasted months and died.

sentences.zh: 王嘉復為丞相，數諫爭忤指。

translations.literal: Wang Jia again became chancellor and several times remonstrated and disputed, disobeying intention.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jia returned as chancellor and defied the throne repeatedly.

sentences.zh: 旬歲間閱三相，議者皆以為不及光。

translations.literal: Within a year three chancellors passed in review; debaters all thought none matched Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Three chancellors fell in a year; everyone agreed none matched Guang.

sentences.zh: 上由是思之。

translations.literal: The emperor on this account thought of him.
translations.idiomatic: Ai began to miss him.

sentences.zh: 會元壽元年正月朔日有蝕之，後十餘日傅太后崩。

translations.literal: It happened that in the first year of Yuanshou, on the new moon of the first month, there was a solar eclipse; a little more than ten days later Grand Empress Dowager Fu died.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshou 1 an eclipse marked the new year; Lady Fu died days later.

sentences.zh: 是月，征光詣公車，問日蝕事。

translations.literal: That month he summoned Guang to the public carriage gate and asked about the solar eclipse affair.
translations.idiomatic: That month Ai summoned Guang to the public carriage office to discuss the eclipse.

sentences.zh: 光對曰：「臣聞日者，眾陽之宗，人君之表，至尊之象。

translations.literal: Guang replied, saying, “Your subject has heard that the sun is the ancestor of the multitude of yang, the model of the lord of men, the image of the most honored.
translations.idiomatic: Guang answered: “The sun is chief of all yang, mirror of the ruler, emblem of supremacy.

sentences.zh: 君德衰微，陰道盛強，侵蔽陽明，則日蝕應之。

translations.literal: When the lord’s virtue wanes and grows slight, the yin way flourishes strong, encroaches and blocks the yang brightness, then solar eclipse responds to it.
translations.idiomatic: When royal virtue fades and yin swells, it swallows the sun.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰『羞用五事』，『建用皇極』。

translations.literal: The Documents says ‘be ashamed in the five affairs,’ ‘establish and use the great mean.’
translations.idiomatic: The Documents speaks of shame in the five pursuits and founding the great mean.

sentences.zh: 如貌、言、視、聽、思失，大中之道不立，則咎徵荐臻，六極屢降。

translations.literal: If in bearing, words, sight, hearing, and thought there is failure, and the way of the great mean is not established, then fault omens come repeatedly and the six extremes descend again and again.
translations.idiomatic: When deportment, speech, sight, hearing, and thought fail, the mean collapses, omens cluster, and the six extremes descend.

sentences.zh: 皇之不極，是為大中不立，其傳曰『時則有日月亂行』，謂朓、側匿，甚則薄蝕是也。

translations.literal: When the sovereign fails the great mean, this is called the great mean not established; its tradition says ‘then there is sun and moon running in disorder,’ meaning excess and concealment, and in the extreme thin eclipse—this is it.
translations.idiomatic: Loss of the mean brings “disordered sun and moon”—irregular motion, then near-extinction of the disk.

sentences.zh: 又曰『六沴之作』，歲之朝曰三朝，其應至重。

translations.literal: It also says ‘when the six excesses act’—the year’s morning is called the three mornings; its response is supremely weighty.
translations.idiomatic: The “six excesses” text adds: the year’s dawn is thrice sacred; its portent weighs heavily.

sentences.zh: 迺正月辛丑朔日有蝕之，變見三朝之會。

translations.literal: Then on xinchou, new moon of the first month, there was a solar eclipse; the change appeared at the meeting of the three mornings.
translations.idiomatic: The xinchou new-year eclipse struck the triple dawn—a grave sign.

sentences.zh: 上天聰明，苟無其事，變不虛生。

translations.literal: High heaven is intelligent; if there were not the affair, the change would not arise in vain.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven does not warn without cause.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰『惟先假王正厥事』，言異變之來，起事有不正也。

translations.literal: The Documents says ‘only first lend the king to correct his affairs,’ meaning when strange changes come, the arising affairs are not correct.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents says heaven lends the king time to mend policy when omens strike.

sentences.zh: 臣聞師曰，天左與王者，故災異數見，以譴告之，欲其改更。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard the teacher say: heaven stands left of the king, therefore disasters and anomalies appear repeatedly to reprove and inform him, wishing him to change and renew.
translations.idiomatic: My teacher said heaven stands at the king’s left, sending omens until he reforms.

sentences.zh: 若不畏懼，有以塞除，而輕忽簡誣，則凶罰加焉，其至可必。

translations.literal: If he does not fear and dread, has means to block and remove them, and treats them lightly, neglectfully, and dismissively, then fierce punishment is added to him—its arrival can be counted on.
translations.idiomatic: If he sneers and slights them, harsh punishment follows as sure as fate.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：『敬之敬之，天惟顯思，命不易哉！』

translations.literal: The Odes says, “Revere it, revere it—heaven makes its brightness clear; the charge is not easy!”
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry warns: “Revere, revere—heaven watches; the mandate is hard.”

sentences.zh: 又曰：『畏天之威，於時保之。』

translations.literal: It also says, “Fear heaven’s majesty, thereby to preserve it.”
translations.idiomatic: It adds: “Fear heaven’s might and you preserve your throne.”

sentences.zh: 皆謂不懼者凶，懼之則吉也。

translations.literal: All mean that those who do not fear meet ill; those who fear meet good.
translations.idiomatic: Contempt brings woe; fear brings safety.

sentences.zh: 陛下聖德聰明，兢兢業業，承順天戒，敬畏變異，勤心虛己，延見群臣，思求其故，然後敕躬自約，總正萬事，放遠讒說之黨，援納斷斷之介，退去貪殘之徒，進用賢良之吏，平刑罰，薄賦斂，恩澤加於百姓，誠為政之大本，應變之至務也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty’s sagely virtue is intelligent; fearful and careful, you receive and follow heaven’s warnings, revere and fear changes and anomalies, diligently mind and empty yourself, extend audience to the assembled ministers, and seek their reasons—then discipline your person with restraint, overall correct the myriad affairs, dismiss far the faction of slanderous persuasion, draw in the upright firmness of the decisive, retire the greedy and cruel throng, advance good and capable clerks, even punishments, lighten levies, extend favor to the hundred surnames—this is truly the great root of governing and the utmost task in answering change.
translations.idiomatic: You are quick and careful, shun the hall, hear ministers, seek causes—then tighten conduct, right every policy, cast off flatterers, lift the upright, sack the cruel, promote the good, ease punishments and taxes, and shower grace on the people—that is how to answer heaven.

sentences.zh: 天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Exceedingly fortunate for all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The realm would be blessed.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰『天既付命正厥德』，言正德以順天也。

translations.literal: The Documents says ‘heaven having given the charge, correct its virtue,’ meaning to correct virtue so as to follow heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents says heaven gives the charge—then straighten virtue to match it.

sentences.zh: 又曰『天棐諶辭』，言有誠道，天輔之也。

translations.literal: It also says ‘heaven supports the sincere declaration,’ meaning where there is the way of sincerity, heaven aids it.
translations.idiomatic: It also says heaven aids the sincere word.

sentences.zh: 明承順天道在於崇德博施，加精至誠，孳孳而已。

translations.literal: To make clear that receiving and following the heavenly way lies in honoring virtue, broadly bestowing, adding utmost sincerity, and working tirelessly alone.
translations.idiomatic: Serving heaven means piling on virtue, giving widely, and striving in utter good faith.

sentences.zh: 俗之祈禳小數，終無益於應天塞異，銷禍興福，較然甚明，無可疑惑。」

translations.literal: The vulgar prayers and minor tallies in the end are of no benefit in answering heaven, blocking anomalies, melting disaster and raising blessing—comparatively this is very clear; there is nothing to doubt.”
translations.idiomatic: Village charms and petty exorcism never answer omens or turn disaster—that is plain beyond doubt.”

sentences.zh: 書奏，上說，賜光束帛，拜為光祿大夫，秩中二千石，給事中，位次丞相。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the emperor was pleased, bestowed bundles of silk on Guang, appointed him Palace Grandee, rank at two thousand piculs in autumn, attendant within the palace, standing next after the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased, Ai gave him silk, made him palace grandee at two thousand piculs, palace attendant, ranked just below the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 詔光舉可尚書令者封上，光謝曰：「臣以朽材，前比歷位典天職，卒無尺寸之效，幸免罪誅，全保首領，今復拔擢，備內朝臣，與聞政事。

translations.literal: An edict ordered Guang to raise one who could be Director of the Masters of Writing and seal and present the name; Guang declined, saying, “Your subject with rotten timber, formerly in succession held posts bearing heaven’s duty, in the end had no effect the size of a foot or inch, luckily escaped crime and execution and wholly preserved head and neck; now again being pulled up and appointed, filling the ranks of inner-court ministers and hearing government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Ordered to name a director of the secretariat, Guang demurred: “I am worn wood; past high posts earned no merit; I barely kept my head; now to join inner government shames me.

sentences.zh: 臣光智謀淺短，犬馬齒臷，誠恐一旦顛仆，無以報稱。

translations.literal: Your subject Guang’s plans and counsel are shallow and short; dog-and-horse teeth are worn—truly I fear that once I stumble and fall I will have no means to repay the charge.”
translations.idiomatic: I am shallow and old; one stumble would leave me unable to repay you.”

sentences.zh: 竊見國家故事，尚書以久次轉遷，非有踔絕之能，不相逾越。

translations.literal: Your subject has privately seen the state’s precedent: the Masters of Writing advance by long seniority in order, and unless there is outstanding ability they do not leap over one another.
translations.idiomatic: By precedent secretariat directors rise by seniority, not leapfrogging.

sentences.zh: 尚書僕射敞，公正勤職，通敏於事，可尚書令。

translations.literal: Vice-Director of the Masters of Writing Chang is fair, upright, diligent in duty, penetrating and quick in affairs—he may be Director of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: Vice-director Cheng Gong Chang is just, diligent, and quick—fit for the post.

sentences.zh: 謹封上。」

translations.literal: I respectfully seal and present this.”
translations.idiomatic: I respectfully submit this name.”

sentences.zh: 敞以舉故，為東平太守。

translations.literal: Chang because of the recommendation became Governor of Dongping.
translations.idiomatic: Chang was sent to govern Dongping on that nomination.

sentences.zh: 敞姓成公，東海人也。

translations.literal: Chang’s surname was Cheng Gong; he was a native of Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Chang’s compound surname was Cheng Gong; he hailed from Donghai.

sentences.zh: 光為大夫月餘，丞相嘉下獄死，御史大夫賈延免。

translations.literal: Guang had been grandee a little more than a month when Chancellor Jia was cast into prison and died, and Imperial Counselor Jia Yan was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: A month later Chancellor Jia died in prison and Imperial Counselor Jia Yan was cashiered.

sentences.zh: 光復為御史大夫，二月為丞相，復故國博山侯。

translations.literal: Guang again became imperial counselor; in the second month he became chancellor, restoring his former state as Marquis of Boshan.
translations.idiomatic: Guang returned as imperial counselor, then in the second month as chancellor, with his old Boshan marquisate restored.

sentences.zh: 上乃知光前免非其罪，以過近臣毀短光者，復免傅嘉，曰：「前為侍中，毀譖仁賢，誣訴大臣，令俊艾者久失其位。

translations.literal: The emperor then knew that Guang’s former dismissal was not for his crime—that through fault of close ministers who slandered and shortened Guang—and again dismissed Fu Jia, saying: “Formerly as palace attendant you slandered benevolent worthies and falsely accused great ministers, causing outstanding men long to lose their posts.
translations.idiomatic: Ai saw Guang’s first fall was wrongful slander; he cashiered Fu Jia, saying, “As attendant you slandered good men and framed high ministers, leaving talent long unemployed.

sentences.zh: 嘉傾覆巧偽，挾奸以罔上，崇黨以蔽朝，傷善以肆意。

translations.literal: Jia overturned and was crafty and false, embraced wickedness to deceive the superior, exalted factions to block the court, injured the good to indulge his will.
translations.idiomatic: Jia was a schemer who built cliques, masked the court, and hurt the worthy to have his way.

sentences.zh: 《詩》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Poetry say?

sentences.zh: 『讒人罔極，交亂四國。』

translations.literal: ‘Slanderers have no limit—they throw the four states into chaos.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Slander knows no limit—it sets the four states at odds.’

sentences.zh: 其免嘉為庶人，歸故郡。」

translations.literal: Let Jia be dismissed to commoner status and returned to his home commandery.”
translations.idiomatic: Strip Jia to commoner rank and send him home.”

sentences.zh: 明年，定三公官，光更為大司徒。

translations.literal: The next year they fixed the Three Dukes offices; Guang was transferred to be Grand Minister of Education.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the three dukes were reorganized and Guang became grand minister of education.

sentences.zh: 會哀帝崩，太皇太后以新都侯王莽為大司馬，征立中山王，是為平帝。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Ai died; the Grand Empress Dowager made Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang grand marshal, summoned and established the King of Zhongshan—this was Emperor Ping.
translations.idiomatic: When Ai died, the grand empress dowager made Wang Mang grand marshal and set the king of Zhongshan on the throne as Emperor Ping.

sentences.zh: 帝年幼，太后稱制，委政於莽。

translations.literal: The emperor was young; the empress dowager held the seals of power and entrusted government to Mang.
translations.idiomatic: The boy emperor sat while the grand empress dowager ruled through Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 初，哀帝罷黜王氏，故太后與莽怨丁、傅、董賢之黨。

translations.literal: At the beginning Emperor Ai had deposed the Wang clan; therefore the empress dowager and Mang resented the factions of Ding, Fu, and Dong Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Ai had cast down the Wangs, so the dowager and Mang hated Ding, Fu, and Dong Xian’s parties.

sentences.zh: 莽以光為舊相名儒，天下所信，太后敬之，備禮事光。

translations.literal: Mang because Guang was a former chancellor and famed Confucian, trusted by all under heaven, and the empress dowager respected him, treated Guang with full ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Mang courted Guang as ex-chancellor and trusted scholar the dowager honored.

sentences.zh: 所欲搏擊，輒為草，以太后指風光令上之，睚眥莫不誅傷。

translations.literal: Whatever he wished to strike down, he would draft for him, using the empress dowager’s intention to hint to Guang to memorialize it upward—not a grudge but that none escaped wounding.
translations.idiomatic: Every purge Mang drafted for Guang to submit in the dowager’s name; none who crossed him went unscathed.

sentences.zh: 莽權日盛，光憂懼不知所出，上書乞骸骨。

translations.literal: As Mang’s power daily grew, Guang worried and feared, not knowing what to do, and submitted a memorial begging leave on bones.
translations.idiomatic: As Mang swelled, Guang lived in dread and begged to retire.

sentences.zh: 莽白太后：「帝幼少，宜置師傅。」

translations.literal: Mang reported to the empress dowager: “The emperor is young and small; it is fitting to set tutors and protectors.”
translations.idiomatic: Mang told the dowager the child needed tutors.

sentences.zh: 徙光為帝太傅，位四輔，給事中，領宿衛供養，行內署門戶，省服御食物。

translations.literal: He transferred Guang to be grand tutor to the emperor, rank among the Four Supports, attendant within the palace, in charge of night guards and provisioning, conducting inner-palace gates, and cutting garments, chariots, and food.
translations.idiomatic: He made Guang imperial grand tutor among the four supports, with night guard, inner gates, and charge of the boy’s household budget.

sentences.zh: 明年，徙為太師，而莽為太傅。

translations.literal: The next year he was transferred to be grand preceptor, while Mang became grand tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Next year Guang became grand preceptor while Mang took the grand tutor title.

sentences.zh: 光常稱疾，不敢與莽並。

translations.literal: Guang often claimed illness and did not dare stand alongside Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Guang feigned illness to avoid standing beside Mang.

sentences.zh: 有詔朝朔望，領城門兵。

translations.literal: There was an edict that he attend court on new and full moons and command the capital gate troops.
translations.idiomatic: An edict kept him for bimonthly audiences and command of the gate guard.

sentences.zh: 莽又風群臣奏莽功德，稱宰衡，位在諸侯王上，百官統焉。

translations.literal: Mang again hinted to the assembled ministers to memorialize Mang’s merit and virtue, styling him “regulating weight” with rank above the feudal kings, all hundred offices answering to him.
translations.idiomatic: Mang had ministers praise him as “regulating weight,” above all kings, with every office under his thumb.

sentences.zh: 光愈恐，固稱疾辭位。

translations.literal: Guang grew more frightened and firmly claimed illness to resign his post.
translations.idiomatic: Guang’s terror grew; he pressed his resignation harder.

sentences.zh: 太后詔曰：「太師光，聖人之後，先師之子，德行純淑，道不通明，居四輔職，輔道於帝。

translations.literal: The empress dowager’s edict said: “Grand Preceptor Guang, scion of the sages, son of the former master, whose virtue and conduct are pure and good and whose learning and the Way are thorough and bright, holds office among the Four Supports and assists and guides the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager replied: “Grand Preceptor Guang, sage heir and fine scholar, pure in conduct and lucid in doctrine, stands among the four supports to teach the emperor.

sentences.zh: 今年耆有疾，俊艾大臣，惟國之重，其猶不可以闕焉。

translations.literal: This year, though aged and ill, as an outstanding great minister and weight of the state, still he cannot be left vacant.
translations.idiomatic: Though old and ailing, he is a pillar of state and cannot be dispensed with.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰『無遺耇老』，國之將興，尊師而重傅。

translations.literal: The Documents says “do not abandon the aged”—when a state is about to rise, it honors the teacher and esteems the tutor.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents forbids casting off the aged; a rising state honors its tutors.

sentences.zh: 其令太師毋朝，十日一賜餐。

translations.literal: Order the grand preceptor not to attend court; every ten days grant him one meal.
translations.idiomatic: Exempt him from daily court; send a court meal every ten days.

sentences.zh: 賜太師靈壽杖，黃門令為太師省中坐置幾，太師入省中用杖，賜餐十七物，然後歸老於第，官屬按職如故。」

translations.literal: Bestow on the grand preceptor the Lingshou staff; let the chief of yellow gates for the grand preceptor set a bench in the palace office; when the grand preceptor enters the palace office he may use the staff; grant meals of seventeen dishes—then return old age to his mansion; his official subordinates carry their duties as before.”
translations.idiomatic: Give him the Lingshou cane, a seat in the secretariat, seventeen dishes per imperial meal, then let him age at home while his staff keep their posts.”

sentences.zh: 光凡為御史大夫、丞相各再，一為大司徒、太傅、太師，歷三世，居公輔位前後十七年。

translations.literal: Guang in all was imperial counselor and chancellor twice each, once grand minister of education, grand tutor, and grand preceptor, passing through three reigns, occupying the highest ministerial places seventeen years front and back.
translations.idiomatic: He served twice as counselor and twice as chancellor, once as grand minister of education, grand tutor, and grand preceptor—three reigns, seventeen years at the top.

sentences.zh: 自為尚書，止不教授，後為卿，時會門下大生講問疑難，舉大義雲。

translations.literal: From the time he was Director of the Masters of Writing he stopped not teaching; later as high minister he would sometimes convene great students of his gate to lecture and ask doubtful points, setting forth the great meanings only.
translations.idiomatic: After the secretariat he ceased regular teaching; as minister he would only sometimes gather senior disciples to clear hard points in broad outline.

sentences.zh: 其弟子多成就為博士、大夫者，見師居大位，幾得其助力，光終無所薦舉，至或怨之。

translations.literal: His disciples many became erudits and grandees; seeing the teacher occupy great position, they nearly obtained his helping force—yet Guang in the end recommended none of them, so that some resented him.
translations.idiomatic: Many pupils rose high and hoped for his pull, but he recommended none—some nursed a grudge.

sentences.zh: 其公如此。

translations.literal: His fairness was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his impartiality.

sentences.zh: 光年七十，元始五年薨。

translations.literal: Guang at age seventy died in the fifth year of Yuanshi.
translations.idiomatic: He died at seventy in Yuanshi 5.

sentences.zh: 莽白太后，使九卿策贈以太師、博山侯印綬，賜乘輿、秘器、金錢、雜帛。

translations.literal: Mang reported to the empress dowager and had the Nine Ministers issue a rescript bestowing the grand preceptor’s and Marquis of Boshan seals and ribbons, granting imperial bier, secret burial goods, gold, cash, and assorted silks.
translations.idiomatic: Mang had the nine ministers award posthumous seals, imperial coffin gear, gold, and silk.

sentences.zh: 少府供張，諫大夫持節與謁者二人使護喪事，博士護行禮。

translations.literal: The Privy Treasurer set out displays; a grandee remonstrant bore credentials with two ushers to oversee the funeral; erudits guarded the performance of rites.
translations.idiomatic: The privy treasurer staged the rites; a grandee remonstrant and ushers supervised; erudits directed ceremony.

sentences.zh: 太后跡遣中謁者持節視喪。

translations.literal: The empress dowager in person sent palace ushers with credentials to view the mourning.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager sent palace ushers to oversee the obsequies.

sentences.zh: 公卿百官會吊送葬。

translations.literal: Dukes, ministers, and the hundred offices gathered to condole and escort the burial.
translations.idiomatic: The whole bureaucracy attended the funeral train.

sentences.zh: 載以乘輿轀輬及副各一乘，羽林孤兒諸生合四百人挽送。

translations.literal: He was loaded in an imperial hearselike carriage and a secondary carriage each one; four hundred forest guardsmen, orphans, and students combined pulled the procession.
translations.idiomatic: His bier rode imperial hearses with a spare coach; four hundred guardsmen, cadets, and students drew the ropes.

sentences.zh: 車萬餘輛，道路皆舉音以過喪。

translations.literal: There were more than ten thousand carts; along the roads all raised voices passing the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: Over ten thousand carts lined the route; mourners wailed along every mile.

sentences.zh: 將作穿復土，可甲卒五百人，起墳如大將軍王鳳制度。

translations.literal: The master of works dug the passage for the lowering; five hundred armored laborers were permitted; the mound was raised by the regulations for Grand General Wang Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Five hundred convict laborers dug the shaft; the tumulus matched Wang Feng’s grand-general standard.

sentences.zh: 謚曰簡烈侯。

translations.literal: His posthumous title was Marquis Jianlie.
translations.idiomatic: His posthumous name was Marquis Jianlie—resolute and ardent.

sentences.zh: 初，光以丞相封，後益封，凡食邑萬一千戶。

translations.literal: At the beginning, when Guang was enfeoffed as chancellor, later with added enfeoffments, in all his revenue households were eleven thousand.
translations.idiomatic: First enfeoffed as chancellor, he eventually held eleven thousand households.

sentences.zh: 疾甚，上書讓還七千戶，及還所賜一第。

translations.literal: When gravely ill he submitted a memorial yielding back seven thousand households and also returning one mansion that had been bestowed.
translations.idiomatic: Dying, he returned seven thousand households and one gifted mansion.

sentences.zh: 初，宮哀帝時與丞相、御史雜議帝祖母傅太后謚，及元始中，王莽發傅太后陵徙歸定陶，以民葬之，追誅前議者。

translations.literal: At the beginning, under Emperor Ai, Gong together with the chancellor and imperial counselor jointly deliberated the posthumous title of the emperor’s grandmother Grand Empress Dowager Fu; when in the Yuanshi period Wang Mang opened Grand Empress Dowager Fu’s tomb, moved her to Dingtao, and buried her as a commoner, he pursued execution of those who had earlier deliberated.
translations.idiomatic: Under Ai, Ma Gong had joined chancellor and counselor in fixing Lady Fu’s posthumous title; when Mang desecrated her tomb and buried her as a commoner, he hunted everyone who had signed that memorial.

sentences.zh: 宮為莽所厚，獨不及，內慚懼，上書謝罪乞骸骨。

translations.literal: Gong was thickly favored by Mang yet alone was not reached; inwardly ashamed and afraid, he submitted a memorial apologizing for crime and begging leave on bones.
translations.idiomatic: Mang spared Ma Gong alone; shamed and frightened, Gong offered to resign.

sentences.zh: 莽以太皇太后詔賜宮策曰：

translations.literal: Mang by the Grand Empress Dowager’s edict bestowed on Gong a rescript saying:
translations.idiomatic: Mang issued a rescript in the dowager’s name:

sentences.zh: 太師、大師徒、扶德侯上書言：「前以光祿勳議故定陶共王母謚，曰『婦人以夫爵尊為號，謚宜曰孝元傅皇后，稱渭陵東園。』

translations.literal: “The Grand Preceptor, Grand Minister of Masses, and Marquis of Fude respectfully memorialized: ‘Formerly, when serving as superintendent of the imperial household, we deliberated the posthumous title of the mother of the former Prince Gong of Dingtao, saying, “For women one honors them by the husband’s rank; the posthumous title should read Empress Xiaoyuan Fu, called east park of Wei Mausoleum.”
translations.idiomatic: “The grand preceptor, the grand minister of masses, and the marquis of Fude wrote: ‘As superintendents of the household we once voted Lady Fu the posthumous title Empress Xiaoyuan Fu, tied to the Wei tomb’s east park.’

sentences.zh: 臣知妾不得體君，卑不得敵尊，而希指雷同，詭經辟說，以惑誤上。

translations.literal: Your subjects knew that a concubine may not match the lord, the low may not rival the high, yet we echoed the hint in chorus, twisted the classics with forced glosses, thereby to confuse and mislead the superior.
translations.idiomatic: We knew a concubine cannot equal a sovereign, nor low match high, yet we toed the line, warped the canon, and misled the throne.

sentences.zh: 為臣不忠，當伏斧鉞之誅，幸蒙灑心自新，又令得保首領。

translations.literal: As ministers we were disloyal and ought to suffer execution by axe and halberd; luckily we were granted cleansing the heart and making ourselves new, and were further allowed to preserve head and neck.
translations.idiomatic: We deserved the axe; we were spared to mend our hearts.

sentences.zh: 伏自惟念，入稱四輔，出備三公，爵為列侯，誠無顏復望闕廷，無心復居官府，無宜復食國邑。

translations.literal: Prostrate, we reflect: entering we were called among the Four Supports, going out we filled the Three Dukes, our nobility was full marquis—we truly have no face again to hope for the palace court, no heart again to dwell in official bureaus, no right again to eat state revenue.
translations.idiomatic: We served as four supports and three dukes, ranked as marquises—we cannot face the court, staff an office, or keep fief.

sentences.zh: 願上太師、大司徒、扶德侯印綬，避賢者路。」

translations.literal: We wish to present the grand preceptor’s, grand minister of masses’s, and Marquis of Fude’s seals and ribbons and clear the road for the worthy.”
translations.idiomatic: We beg to surrender the grand preceptor, grand minister of masses, and Fude marquis seals and yield to better men.”

sentences.zh: 下君章有司，皆以為四輔之職為國維綱，三公之任鼎足承君，不有鮮明固守，無以居位。

translations.literal: It was sent down to your excellency’s document and the responsible officials; all thought that the duty of the Four Supports is the state’s main ropes, the charge of the Three Dukes is the tripod feet bearing the lord—without bright steadfastness there is no means to occupy the post.
translations.idiomatic: Officials ruled that four supports and three dukes anchor the state—without firm virtue none may hold those posts.

sentences.zh: 如君言至誠可聽，惟君之惡在灑心前，不敢文過，朕甚多之，不奪君之爵邑，以著「自古皆有死」之義。

translations.literal: If your words, sir, in utmost sincerity may be heard, only your fault lies in cleansing the heart beforehand, not daring to gloss over fault—We greatly approve of this; We will not strip your, sir, noble rank and fief, thereby to display the meaning of “from antiquity all have died.”
translations.idiomatic: Your candor moves us; your fault was paid in a cleansed heart, not hidden—we praise that and leave your title and fief to show that all men owe death.

sentences.zh: 其上太師、大司徒印綬使者，以侯就第。

translations.literal: Present the grand preceptor’s and grand minister of masses’s seals and ribbons to the envoy; as marquis go to your mansion.”
translations.idiomatic: Return the grand preceptor and grand minister of masses seals to the envoy; keep the marquisate and retire.”

sentences.zh: 王莽篡位，以宮為太子師，卒官。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang usurped the throne, he made Gong tutor to the heir apparent; he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: When Mang seized the throne he made Gong tutor to the heir; Gong died in that post.

sentences.zh: 本姓馬矢，宮仕學，稱馬氏雲。

translations.literal: His original surname was Ma Shi (“horse-arrow”); when Gong entered office and study he styled himself the Ma clan, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Born Ma Shi, he shortened the name to Ma when he entered official life.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：自孝武興學，公孫弘以儒相，其後蔡義、韋賢、玄成、匡衡、張禹、翟方進、孔光、平當、馬宮及當子晏咸以儒宗居宰相位，服儒衣冠，傳先王語，其醞藉可也，然皆持祿保位，被阿諛之譏。

translations.literal: The eulogy says: From the time Emperor Xiaowu raised learning, Gongsun Hong as Confucian became chancellor; afterward Cai Yi, Wei Xian, Xuancheng, Kuang Heng, Zhang Yu, Zhai Fangjin, Kong Guang, Ping Dang, Ma Gong, and Dang’s son Yan all as Confucian patriarchs occupied the chancellor’s seat, wore Confucian cap and gown, transmitted the former kings’ words—their cultivation may pass muster, yet all held salary and guarded position and bore the ridicule of flattery.
translations.idiomatic: The summation runs: since Wu promoted the classics, Gongsun Hong and the long line of Ru chancellors—Cai, Wei, Xuancheng, Kuang, Zhang, Zhai, Kong, Ping, Ma, and Yan—wore scholar dress and quoted the kings; they had polish, yet clung to pay and earned the name of sycophants.

sentences.zh: 彼以古人之跡見繩，烏能勝其任乎！

translations.literal: If they are measured by the tracks of the ancients, how can they bear the burden!
translations.idiomatic: Measured against the ancients, how could they bear the weight?
